questionText: The function $f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}$ has a local minima at $x$ equal to
subject: Mathematics
solution:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \text { Put, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x= \pm 2 \\ & \text { Now, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{6}{x^3} \\ & \text { At } x=2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(2)=\frac{6}{8}>0 \\ & \text { At } x=-2, \quad f^{\prime \prime}(-2)=-\frac{6}{8}<0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Thus, } f(x) \text { has local minima at } x=2 \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Given a curve $y=7 x-x^3$ and $x$ increases at the rate of 2 units per second. The rate at which the slope of the curve is changing, when $x=5$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given, }\\ &y=7 x-x^3 \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=7-3 x^2$$

Thus, slope, $\quad s=7-3 x^2$

Now, differentiating w.r.t. ' $t$ ', we get

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \frac{d s}{d t} & =-6 x \frac{d x}{d t} \\ \therefore & \left.\frac{d s}{d t}\right|_{\text {at } x=5} & =-6 \times 5 \times 2 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad\left[\because \frac{d x}{d t}=2 \text { units } / \mathrm{s} \text { (given) }\right] \\ & =-60 \mathrm{units} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: If $x e^y=1$, then the value of $\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $x=1$ is
subject: Mathematics

questionText: Derivative of $e^{\sin ^2 x}$ with respect to $\cos x$ is
options:
$\sin x e^{\sin ^2 x}$
 $\cos x e^{\sin ^2 x}$
 $-2 \cos x e^{\sin ^2 x}$
 $-2 \sin ^2 x \cos x e^{\sin ^2 x}$
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $\quad u=e^{\sin ^2 x}$ and $v=\cos x$

$\therefore \quad \frac{d u}{d x}=e^{\sin ^2 x}(2 \sin x) \cos x$

and $$\frac{d v}{d x}=-\sin x$$

Thus,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d u}{d v} & =\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x} \\ & =\frac{e^{\sin ^2 x} \cdot 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}{-\sin x} \\ & =-2 \cos x e^{\sin ^2 x} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: If $A$ is a square matrix of order 3 such that the value of $\mid$ adj. $A \mid=8$, then the value of $\left|\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}\right|$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that

$|\operatorname{adj} A|=|A|^{n-1}$

where $n$ is the order of square matrix $A$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} \text { Given } & & |\operatorname{adj} A| & =8 \\ \therefore & & |A|^{n-1} & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A|^{3-1} & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A|^2 & =8 \\ \Rightarrow & & |A| & =2 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$$

Also, determinant of a matrix and its transpose has same values.

questionText: If inverse of matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$ is the matrix $\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4\end{array}\right]$ then value of $\lambda$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

$$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]$$

and $$A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]$$

Since, $A A^{-1}=I$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 12-3 \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & -3+\lambda & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing matrices, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 12-3 \lambda=0 \text { and }-3+\lambda=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=4 \text { and } \lambda=4 \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The area of the circle is increasing at a uniform rate of $2 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s}$. How fast is the circumference of the circle increasing when the radius $r=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let radius of the circle be } r \mathrm{~cm} \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Given } & \frac{d A}{d t} & =2 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\ & \text { since } & A & =\pi r^2 \\ & \therefore & \frac{d A}{d t} & =2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Also, circumference, } C=2 \pi r\\ &\therefore \quad \frac{d C}{d t}=2 \pi \frac{d r}{d t}\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} \text { from (i), } & 2 =2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d r}{d t} =\frac{1}{\pi r} \end{array}$$

Now, substituting the value of $\frac{d r}{d t}$ in eq. (ii) we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d c}{d t} & =2 \pi \frac{1}{\pi r}=\frac{2}{r} \\ \text{Now,}\quad\left.\frac{d C}{d t}\right|_{\text {at } r=5} & =\frac{2}{5} \\ & =0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, circumference of circle increases at the rate of $0.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$.

questionText: If $x=a \sin ^3 \theta, y=b \cos ^3 \theta$,then find $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$ at $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given, $x=a \sin ^3 \theta$ and $y=a \cos ^3 \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \\ & \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta \\ & \text { and } \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 a \cos ^2 \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =-\cot \theta \\ & \text { and } \quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(-\cot \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d x} \\ & =\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 a \sin ^4 \theta \cos \theta} \\ & \text { Now, }\left.\quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right|_{\text {at } x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{3 \cdot a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^4 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3 \cdot a \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3 a} \end{aligned}$$

solution:

Area of printed part $=x y$

Now, area of visiting card $=(x+3)(y+2)$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)(y+2) \\ \text{or, }\quad & A=(x+3)\left(\frac{24}{x}+2\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{(x+3)(24+2 x)}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=\frac{2 x^2+30 x+72}{x} \\ \text { or, }\quad & A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (ii) We have, Area, } A=2 x+30+\frac{72}{x}\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d A}{d x}=2+0-\frac{72}{x^2} \\ \text { and } & \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}=\frac{144}{x^3} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

for minimum area, put $\frac{d A}{d x}=0$

i.e., $$2-\frac{72}{x^2}=0$$

or, $$x^2=36 \Rightarrow x= \pm 6$$

At $x=6 \cdot \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2}>0$ Hence, minimum

Therefore, length of vising card

$$\begin{aligned} & =x+3=6+3=9 \mathrm{~cm} \\ \text { and breadth of visiting card }\quad & =y+2=\frac{24}{x}+2 \\ & =\frac{24}{6}+2=6 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Find the area of the region bounded by the curve $4 x^2$ $+y^2=36$ using integration.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given curve is $4 x^2+y^2=36$

or, $\quad \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{36}=1$

or, $\quad \frac{x^2}{3^2}+\frac{y^2}{6^2}=1$

since, ellipse is symmetrical along

$x$-axis and $y$-axis

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Area of ellipse } & =\text { Area of ABCD } \\ & =4 \times \text { Area of OBC } \\ & =4 \times \int_0^3 y d x \\ & =4 \times \int_0^3\left(2 \sqrt{9-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =8 \int_0^3 \sqrt{9-x^2} d x \end{aligned}$$

[since OBC is above $x$-axis]

$$=8 \int_0^3 \sqrt{3^2-x^2} d x$$

$$=8\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{3^2-x^2}+\frac{3^2}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_0^3$$

$$=8\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_0^3$$

$$=8\left[0+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1}(1)-(0+0)\right]$$

$$=8 \times \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$=2 \times 9 \pi$$

$$=18 \pi \text { sq. units }$$

questionText: $\int \frac{1}{x(\log x)^2} d x$ is equal to
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \quad I=\int \frac{1}{x(\log x)^2}=d x \\ & \log x=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x} d x=d t \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & I=\int \frac{d t}{t^2}=-\frac{1}{t}+C \\ \text { Thus, } & I=-\frac{1}{\log x}+C \end{array}$$

questionText: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}a & c & -1 \\ b & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -5 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a skew-symmetric matrix, then the value of $2 a-(b+c)$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that, if matrix A is skew symmetric, then

$$\begin{aligned} A^{\prime} & =-A \\ \therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{ccc} a & b & 1 \\ c & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -a & -c & 1 \\ -b & 0 & -5 \\ -1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

On comparing matrices, we get

$$\begin{aligned} a & =-a \Rightarrow 2 a=0 \Rightarrow a=0 \\ b & =-c \text { and } c=-b \end{aligned}$$

Now, $\quad 2 a+(b+c)=2 \times 0+(b-b)=0$

questionText: What is the greatest value of f(x)?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
First, compute the derivative and find critical points:

f ′(x)=d/dx[cos2x + x]=−2 sin2x + 1.

Set f ′(x)=0 ⇒ 1−2 sin2x=0 ⇒ sin2x=½

⇒ 2x=π/6 or 5π/6 ⇒ x=π/12 or 5π/12.

Next, evaluate f(x)=cos2x + x at these and at the endpoints x=±π/2:

At x=π/12:

$$f\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{12}\bigr)=\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{6}\bigr)+\tfrac{\pi}{12} =\frac{\sqrt3}{2}+\frac{\pi}{12}\approx1.128.$$

At x=5π/12:

$$f\bigl(\tfrac{5\pi}{12}\bigr)=\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{5\pi}{6}\bigr)+\tfrac{5\pi}{12} =-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}+\frac{5\pi}{12}\approx0.443.$$

At x=π/2:

$$f\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr)=\cos\pi+\tfrac{\pi}{2}=-1+\tfrac{\pi}{2}\approx0.571.$$

At x=−π/2:

$$f\bigl(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr)=\cos(-\pi)-\tfrac{\pi}{2}=-1-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\approx-2.571.$$

The largest value is

$$\frac{\sqrt3}{2}+\frac{\pi}{12},$$

so Option 2 is correct.

questionText: What is the least value of f(x)?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let’s find the minimum of

$$f(x)=\cos2x+x\,,\qquad x\in\bigl[-\tfrac\pi2,\tfrac\pi2\bigr].$$

Compute the derivative:

$$f'(x)=-2\sin2x+1=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\sin2x=\tfrac12.$$

This gives interior critical points

$$x=\frac\pi{12}\quad\text{and}\quad x=\frac{5\pi}{12}$$

(both lie in $[-\tfrac\pi2,\tfrac\pi2]$).

Evaluate $f$ at the critical points and the endpoints:

$x=-\tfrac\pi2:\;f(-\tfrac\pi2)=\cos(-\pi)-\tfrac\pi2=-1-\tfrac\pi2$

$x=\tfrac\pi{12}:\;f(\tfrac\pi{12})=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}+\tfrac\pi{12}>0$

$x=\tfrac{5\pi}{12}:\;f(\tfrac{5\pi}{12})=-\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}+\tfrac{5\pi}{12}>0$

$x=\tfrac\pi2:\;f(\tfrac\pi2)=-1+\tfrac\pi2>0$

The smallest value is

$$f\bigl(-\tfrac\pi2\bigr)=-1-\tfrac\pi2\,, $$

which matches Option 1.

questionText: What is the value of k?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Explanation:

Given:

The area bounded by the parabola $ y^2 = kx $ and the line $ x = k $, where $ k > 0 $, is $ \frac{4}{3} $ square units.

The equation for the area bounded is:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k y \, dx $

Substituting $ y $ from the parabola equation $ y^2 = kx $, we have:

$ y = \sqrt{kx} $

So the integral becomes:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k \sqrt{k} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

This simplifies to:

$ 1 = k^2 $

Solving for $ k $, we find:

$ k = \pm 1 $

Since $ k > 0 $, the value of $ k $ is:

$ k = 1 $

Therefore, the value of $ k $ is 1.

questionText: What is the area of the parabola bounded by the latus rectum?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Explanation:

Given:

The area bounded by the parabola $ y^2 = kx $ and the line $ x = k $, where $ k > 0 $, is $ \frac{4}{3} $ square units.

$ \text{Area bounded} = \frac{4}{3} \, \text{sq. units} $

$ = 2 \int_0^k y \, dx $

Given:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k \sqrt{k} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

Therefore, solving the equation:

$ 1 = k^2 $

Since $ k > 0 $, $ k = 1 $.

Thus, the equation of the parabola becomes $ y^2 = x $.

For the parabola, $ 4a = 1 $:

$ a = \frac{1}{4} $

The area of the parabola $ y^2 = x $ bounded by the latus rectum at $ x = \frac{1}{4} $ is:

$ = 2 \int_0^{\frac{1}{4}} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

$ = 2 \times \frac{2}{3} \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} $

$ = \frac{1}{6} \, \text{sq. units} $

∴ The correct area is $\frac{1}{6}$ square units.

questionText: In the expansion of (1 + x)<sup>p</sup> (1 + x)<sup>q</sup>, if the coefficient of x<sup>3</sup> is 35, then what is the value of (p + q)?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
First note that

$$(1+x)^p\,(1+x)^q=(1+x)^{\,p+q}$$

so the coefficient of $x^3$ is

$$\binom{p+q}{3} \;=\;35.$$

That means

$$\frac{(p+q)(p+q-1)(p+q-2)}{6}=35.$$

Try $p+q=n$:

If $n=7$, then $\tfrac{7\cdot6\cdot5}{6}=35$.

Hence

$ p+q=7. $

Answer: Option 3 (7).

questionText: What is the remainder when 7<sup>n </sup>- 6n is divided by 36 for n = 100?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the remainder of $ 7^n - 6n $ when divided by 36 for $ n = 100 $, consider the expression:

$ p(n) = 7^n - 6n $

This can be rewritten as:

$ p(n) = (6 + 1)^n - 6n $

Using the binomial expansion, we have:

$ = 1 + 6n + \binom{n}{2}6^2 + \binom{n}{3}6^3 + \ldots + 6^n - 6n $

This simplifies to:

$ = 1 + 6^2\left(\binom{n}{2} + \binom{n}{3} \times 6 + \ldots + 6^{n-2}\right) $

For $ n = 100 $:

$ = 1 + 36\left(\binom{100}{2} + \binom{100}{3} \times 6 + \ldots + 6^{98}\right) $

Thus, the expression becomes:

$ p(100) = 1 + 36k $

Where $ k $ is some integer determined by the combinations and powers of 6.

Therefore, when $ 7^n - 6n $ is divided by 36, the remainder is:

$ \boxed{1} $

questionText: <font style="vertical-align: inherit;"><font style="vertical-align: inherit;">What is V + W equal to? </font></font>
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Explanation:

Given:

$ (8 + 3\sqrt{7})^{20} = U + V \quad \text{...(i)} $

$ (8 - 3\sqrt{7})^{20} = W \quad \text{...(ii)} $

Where $ U $ is an integer, $ 0 < V < 1 $, and $ 0 < W < 1 $.

Adding Equations (i) and (ii):

$ U + V + W = (8 + 3\sqrt{7})^{20} + (8 - 3\sqrt{7})^{20} $

Expanding the expression using the binomial theorem:

$ = 2 \left[ \binom{20}{0} \cdot 8^{20} + \binom{20}{2} \cdot 8^{18} \cdot (3\sqrt{7})^2 + \ldots + (3\sqrt{7})^{20} \right] $

Since the binomial expansion results in an even number:

$ U $ is an integer.

Given that $ 0 < V < 1 $ and $ 0 < W < 1 $:

$ V + W $ must be an integer.

Thus, we conclude that:

$ V + W = 1 $

Therefore, option (d) is correct.

questionText: <font style="vertical-align: inherit;"><font style="vertical-align: inherit;">What is the value of (U + V)W? </font></font>
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Here’s a quick way to see it:

Note that

$$ (8+3\sqrt7)(8-3\sqrt7) = 8^2 - (3\sqrt7)^2 = 64 - 63 = 1. $$

Raising both sides to the 20th power gives

$$ (8+3\sqrt7)^{20}\,(8-3\sqrt7)^{20} = 1^{20} = 1. $$

By definition,

$$(8+3\sqrt7)^{20} = U+V,\quad (8-3\sqrt7)^{20} = W,$$

so

$$(U+V)\,W = 1.$$

Answer: 1.

questionText:
The equation of a circle is 

(x2 - 4x + 3) + (y2 - 6y + 8) = 0

Which of the following statements are correct?

I. The end points of a diameter of the circle are at (1, 2) and (3, 4).

II. The end points of a diameter of the circle are at (1, 4) and (3, 2).

III. The end points of a diameter of the circle are at (2, 4) and (4, 2).

Select the answer using the code given below.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
First, rewrite the circle in standard form by completing the square:

• For x:

$$x^2 - 4x + 3 = (x^2 - 4x + 4) + (3 - 4) = (x-2)^2 - 1$$

• For y:

$$y^2 - 6y + 8 = (y^2 - 6y + 9) + (8 - 9) = (y-3)^2 - 1$$

So the given equation

$$(x^2 - 4x + 3) + (y^2 - 6y + 8) = 0$$

becomes

$$(x-2)^2 - 1 \;+\;(y-3)^2 - 1 = 0$$

or

$$(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = 2\,. $$

That’s a circle with center $C=(2,3)$ and radius $r=\sqrt2$.

A pair of points $(x_1,y_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2)$ is a diameter if

Their midpoint is $(2,3)$.

The distance between them is $2r = 2\sqrt2$, so squared distance $=8$.

Check each:

I. $(1,2)$ & $(3,4)$

Midpoint $\bigl(\frac{1+3}2,\frac{2+4}2\bigr)=(2,3)$

Squared distance $(3-1)^2+(4-2)^2=4+4=8$

⇒ valid diameter.

II. $(1,4)$ & $(3,2)$

Midpoint $\bigl(\frac{1+3}2,\frac{4+2}2\bigr)=(2,3)$

Squared distance $(3-1)^2+(2-4)^2=4+4=8$

⇒ valid diameter.

III. $(2,4)$ & $(4,2)$

Midpoint $\bigl(\frac{2+4}2,\frac{4+2}2\bigr)=(3,3)\neq(2,3)$

⇒ not a diameter.

So only I and II are correct.

Answer: Option 1 (I and II only).

questionText: What is BAC equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
A triangle $ \triangle ABC $ is inscribed in the circle described by the equation $ x^2 + y^2 = 100 $. The coordinates for points $ B $ and $ C $ are $ (6, 8) $ and $ (-8, 6) $ respectively.

To determine the angle $ \angle BAC $, we need to consider the following:

Calculation:

Distance $ BC $:

$ BC = \sqrt{(6 + 8)^2 + (8 - 6)^2} = \sqrt{200} = 10\sqrt{2} $.

Circle Center and Radius:

The circle's center $ O $ is at the origin, with a radius $ r = \sqrt{100} = 10 $.

$ OB $ and $ OC $ are both radii of the circle, hence $ 10 $ units each.

Verification of $ \triangle BOC $:

In $ \triangle BOC $, we have:

$ OB^2 + OC^2 = 100 + 100 = 200 $

This simplifies to:

$ BC^2 = (10\sqrt{2})^2 = 200 $

Since $ OB^2 + OC^2 = BC^2 $, $ \triangle BOC $ is a right triangle. Therefore, $ \angle BOC = 90^\circ $ due to the converse of the Pythagorean theorem.

Angle $ \angle BAC $:

Since $ \angle BOC = 90^\circ $, the angle subtended by the chord at the center is twice the angle subtended at any point on the circle, including $ A $.

Therefore:

$ \angle BAC = \frac{1}{2} \times 90^\circ = 45^\circ \quad \text{or} \quad 180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ $

In radians, this translates to:

$ \angle BAC = \frac{\pi}{4} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{3\pi}{4} $

Thus, the measures of $ \angle BAC $ are $\frac{\pi}{4}$ or $\frac{3\pi}{4}$.

questionText: If ω ≠ 1 is a cube root of unity, then what is (1 + ω - ω<sup>2</sup>)<sup>100</sup> + (1 - ω + ω<sup>2</sup>)<sup>100</sup> equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Explanation:

The expression is $(1+\omega-\omega^2)^{100} + (1-\omega+\omega^2)^{100}$.

Given that $\omega$ is a cube root of unity and not equal to 1, we know:

$ 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0 $

This implies:

$ \omega^2 = -1 - \omega $

Now, we evaluate each part of the expression:

First, simplify $ (1 + \omega - \omega^2)^{100} $:

$ 1 + \omega - \omega^2 = 1 + \omega + (1 + \omega) = 2 + 2\omega = -\omega^2 - \omega^2 = -2\omega^2 $

Next, simplify $ (1 - \omega + \omega^2)^{100} $:

$ 1 - \omega + \omega^2 = 1 - \omega + (-1 - \omega) = -2\omega $

Thus, the expression simplifies to:

$ (-2\omega^2)^{100} + (-2\omega)^{100} $

We can extract powers of $\omega$:

Since $(\omega^2)^{100} = \omega^{200}$ and $(\omega)^{100} = \omega^{100}$, and knowing $\omega^3 = 1$, it follows:

$ \omega^{200} = (\omega^3)^{66} \cdot \omega^2 = 1^{66} \cdot \omega^2 = \omega^2 $

$ \omega^{100} = (\omega^3)^{33} \cdot \omega^1 = 1^{33} \cdot \omega = \omega $

This leads to:

$ = 2^{100} (\omega^2 + \omega) $

Given $ 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$ implies $ \omega^2 + \omega = -1 $, therefore:

$ = 2^{100} \times (-1) = -2^{100} $

Thus, the correct result is $-2^{100}$.

questionText: The non-negative values of $b$ for which the function $\frac{16x^3}{3} - 4bx^2 + x$ has neither maximum nor minimum in the range $x>0$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
A function has neither a maximum nor minimum in an interval if its derivative is either always positive or always negative in that interval.

Let's find the derivative of the given function:

$$f'(x) = 16x^2 - 8bx + 1$$

For the derivative to be always positive or always negative, the discriminant of the quadratic expression must be negative. This is because if the discriminant is positive, the quadratic has two distinct real roots, meaning the derivative changes sign and the function has a maximum or minimum.

The discriminant of the quadratic is:

$$D = (-8b)^2 - 4 \cdot 16 \cdot 1 = 64b^2 - 64$$

We want this discriminant to be negative:

$$64b^2 - 64 < 0$$

Dividing both sides by 64:

$$b^2 - 1 < 0$$

Factoring:

$$(b - 1)(b + 1) < 0$$

This inequality holds when $b$ lies between -1 and 1. However, we are only interested in non-negative values of $b$. Therefore, the solution is:

$$0 < b < 1$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.

questionText: What is $\varphi(a)$ equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine what $\varphi(a)$ is equal to, let's analyze the given function:

$$\varphi(a) = \int_{a}^{a + 100 \pi} |\sin x| dx$$

First, consider the behavior and period of the sine function. The function $\sin x$ is periodic with a period of $2 \pi$. Therefore, $|\sin x|$ also repeats every $2 \pi$.

Given that the integral is over an interval of length $100 \pi$, we can express this in terms of the periods of $|\sin x|$:

The interval length is $\frac{100 \pi}{2 \pi} = 50$ periods.

Next, consider the integral over a single period of $|\sin x|$. Since $|\sin x|$ is symmetric and periodic over $[0, 2 \pi]$, we can simplify the computation:

Let's calculate the integral over one period:

$$\int_{0}^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx$$

We know that:

$$\int_0^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx = 2 \int_0^{\pi} \sin x dx$$

The integral of $\sin x$ from $0$ to $\pi$ is:

$$\int_0^{\pi} \sin x dx = [-\cos x]_0^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Thus:

$$\int_{0}^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$$

Each period of $|\sin x|$ contributes $4$ units to the integral. Since there are $50$ periods within our interval, the total integral is:

$$\varphi(a) = 50 \cdot 4 = 200$$

So, $\varphi(a)$ is a constant value of $200$, regardless of $a$.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D

200

questionText: What is $\varphi'(a)$ equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine $\varphi'(a)$, let's start by understanding what the function $\varphi(a)$ represents. It is given by

$$\varphi(a) = \int_{a}^{a + 100\pi} |\sin x| \, dx$$

We need to find the derivative of this function with respect to $a$. To do this, we can use the Leibniz rule for differentiation under the integral sign, which states that if you have an integral of the form

$$\int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} f(x) \, dx$$

then the derivative with respect to $a$ is given by

$$\frac{d}{da} \left( \int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} f(x) \, dx \right) = f(v(a)) \cdot \frac{d}{da}v(a) - f(u(a)) \cdot \frac{d}{da}u(a) + \int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} \frac{\partial}{\partial a} f(x, a) \, dx$$

In our case:

  • $u(a) = a$
  • $v(a) = a + 100\pi$
  • $f(x) = |\sin x|$ (independent of $a$)

Since $f(x)$ is independent of $a$, the partial derivative $\frac{\partial}{\partial a} f(x, a)$ is zero. Therefore, the derivative simplifies to

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(v(a))| \cdot v'(a) - |\sin(u(a))| \cdot u'(a)$$

Substitute $v(a) = a + 100\pi$ and $u(a) = a$:

  • $v'(a) = 1$
  • $u'(a) = 1$

Therefore, we get:

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(a + 100\pi)| \cdot 1 - |\sin(a)| \cdot 1$$

Since $\sin(x)$ has a period of $2\pi$, we know that adding $100\pi$ effectively cycles through the sine function 50 times (because $100\pi$ is $50 \cdot 2\pi$). Hence, $\sin(a + 100\pi) = \sin(a)$. This means that:

$$|\sin(a + 100\pi)| = |\sin(a)|$$

Substituting this in, we get:

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(a)| - |\sin(a)| = 0$$

Thus, $\varphi'(a) = 0$.

The correct answer is:

Option A: 0

questionText:
Which of the following is/are correct?

1. $f'(0) = 0$

2. $f''(0) < 0$

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let's analyze the given statements one by one to determine which of them are correct.

Statement 1: $ f'(0) = 0 $

This statement is based on the condition that $ f(x) $ has a local maximum at $ x = 0 $. For a function to have a local maximum at a point, its first derivative at that point must be zero. Thus, we have:

$$ f'(0) = 0 $$

So, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: $ f''(0) < 0 $

For a point $ x = 0 $ to be a local maximum, not only must the first derivative be zero, but the second derivative at that point must be negative. This indicates that the function changes concavity and thus confirms a local maximum:

$$ f''(0) < 0 $$

So, Statement 2 is also correct.

Therefore, both statements 1 and 2 are correct. The correct answer is:

Option C: Both 1 and 2

questionText: The function $y$ has a relative maxima at $x = 0$ for
subject: Mathematics
solution:
First, let's break down the problem. We are given a differentiable function $ f(x) $ that has a local maximum at $ x = 0 $. This means that at $ x = 0 $, the first derivative of $ f(x) $, $ f'(x) $, is zero, and the second derivative, $ f''(x) $, is less than or equal to zero (because we have a local maximum).

Now, let us consider the function $ y = 2f(x) + ax - b $. To find the conditions under which $ y $ has a relative maximum at $ x = 0 $, we need to look at the first and second derivatives of $ y $ and apply the conditions for a maximum.

The first derivative of $ y $ with respect to $ x $ is:

$$ y' = \frac{d}{dx}(2f(x) + ax - b) = 2f'(x) + a $$

At $ x = 0 $, for $ y $ to have a relative maximum, we need $ y'(0) = 0 $. So, we set:

$$ y'(0) = 2f'(0) + a = 0 $$

Since $ f(x) $ has a local maximum at $ x = 0 $, we know that $ f'(0) = 0 $. Therefore:

$$ 2(0) + a = 0 $$

This simplifies to:

$$ a = 0 $$

Next, let’s check the second derivative of $ y $, $ y'' $, to confirm it is less than 0 at $ x = 0 $ to confirm a local maximum:

$$ y'' = \frac{d^2}{dx^2}(2f(x) + ax - b) = 2f''(x) $$

At $ x = 0 $, we need:

$$ y''(0) = 2f''(0) $$

Since $ f(x) $ has a local maximum at $ x = 0 $, we know that $ f''(0) \leq 0 $. Therefore,

$$ y''(0) = 2f''(0) \leq 0 $$

This means that as long as $ a = 0 $, the conditions for a relative maximum are automatically satisfied, regardless of the value of $ b $.

Hence, the correct option is:

Option B: for all $b$ and $a = 0$

questionText: What is the coefficient of $x^{10}$ in the expansion of $(1-x^2)^{20}\left(2-x^2-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)^{-5}$?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the coefficient of $ x^{10} $ in the expansion of the expression:

$ (1 - x^2)^{20} \left( 2 - x^2 - \frac{1}{x^2} \right)^{-5} $

we need to simplify and manipulate the expression carefully.

Step 1: Simplify the Second Factor

First, rewrite the second factor to make it easier to handle:

$ 2 - x^2 - \frac{1}{x^2} = \frac{2x^2 - x^4 - 1}{x^2} $

Now, write the entire expression:

$ (1 - x^2)^{20} \left( \frac{2x^2 - x^4 - 1}{x^2} \right)^{-5} $

Step 2: Simplify the Denominator

Notice that $ 2x^2 - x^4 - 1 = - (x^4 - 2x^2 + 1) $, which factors further:

$ - (x^4 - 2x^2 + 1) = - (x^2 - 1)^2 $

So, the expression becomes:

$ (1 - x^2)^{20} \left( \frac{ - (x^2 - 1)^2 }{ x^2 } \right)^{-5} $

Step 3: Simplify the Negative Exponent

Apply the negative exponent:

$ \left( \frac{ - (x^2 - 1)^2 }{ x^2 } \right)^{-5} = \left( -1 \right)^{-5} x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{-10} $

Since $ (-1)^{-5} = -1 $, the expression simplifies to:

$ - x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{-10} $

Step 4: Combine with the First Factor

Now, combine this with the first factor:

$ (1 - x^2)^{20} \times \left( - x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{-10} \right) = - x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{20} (x^2 - 1)^{-10} = - x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{10} $

Step 5: Expand $ (x^2 - 1)^{10} $

Use the binomial theorem to expand:

$ (x^2 - 1)^{10} = \sum_{k=0}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (x^2)^{10 - k} (-1)^k $

Step 6: Multiply and Find the Coefficient

Multiply by $ - x^{10} $:

$ - x^{10} (x^2 - 1)^{10} = - \sum_{k=0}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (-1)^k x^{10} \cdot x^{2(10 - k)} = - \sum_{k=0}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (-1)^k x^{30 - 2k} $

We are interested in the term where the exponent of $ x $ is 10:

$ 30 - 2k = 10 \implies 2k = 20 \implies k = 10 $

Step 7: Calculate the Coefficient for $ k = 10 $

Compute the coefficient:

$ \text{Coefficient} = - \binom{10}{10} (-1)^{10} = -1 \times 1 = -1 $

Answer:

$\boxed{-1}$

questionText: If the 4th term in the expansion of $\left(mx + \frac{1}{x}\right)^n$ is $\frac{5}{2}$, then what is the value of $mn$?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the value of $ mn $, we start with the general term in the expansion of $ \left(mx + \frac{1}{x}\right)^n $, given by the binomial theorem:

$$ T_{k+1} = \binom{n}{k} \left(mx\right)^{n-k} \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^k $$

We need to find the 4th term in this expansion, so let $ k = 3 $. Substituting $ k = 3 $ into the general term formula, we get:

$$ T_4 = \binom{n}{3} \left(mx\right)^{n-3} \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^3 $$

Simplify the expression:

$$ T_4 = \binom{n}{3} m^{n-3} x^{n-3} \cdot \frac{1}{x^3} $$

Combine the exponents of $ x $:

$$ T_4 = \binom{n}{3} m^{n-3} x^{n-3-3} = \binom{n}{3} m^{n-3} x^{n-6} $$

Given that the 4th term is $ \frac{5}{2} $, this implies:

$$ \binom{n}{3} m^{n-3} x^{n-6} = \frac{5}{2} $$

For the term to be a constant (i.e., not dependent on $ x $), the exponent of $ x $ must be zero:

$$ n - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow n = 6 $$

Substitute $ n = 6 $ into the equation for the 4th term:

$$ T_4 = \binom{6}{3} m^{6-3} x^0 = \frac{5}{2} $$

This simplifies to:

$$ \binom{6}{3} m^3 = \frac{5}{2} $$

Calculate $ \binom{6}{3} $:

$$ \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3!3!} = 20 $$

Substitute $ \binom{6}{3} $ into the equation:

$$ 20 m^3 = \frac{5}{2} $$

Solve for $ m^3 $:

$$ m^3 = \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{1}{20} = \frac{5}{40} = \frac{1}{8} $$

Taking the cube root of both sides, we find:

$$ m = \frac{1}{2} $$

Finally, calculate $ mn $:

$$ mn = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) \times 6 = 3 $$

Therefore, the value of $ mn $ is 3. The correct option is:

Option B

3

questionText: In a binomial expansion of $(x+y)^{2 n+1}(x-y)^{2 n+1}$, the sum of middle terms is zero. What is the value of $\left(\frac{x^2}{y^2}\right)$ ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The expression we need to expand is $$(x + y)^{2n+1}(x - y)^{2n+1}$$. To find the product, we can use the formula for the binomial expansion of two binomial expressions.

To make it simpler, let us first consider individual expansions:

The binomial expansion of $$(x + y)^{2n+1}$$ is:

$$ (x + y)^{2n+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{2n+1} \binom{2n+1}{k} x^{2n+1-k} y^k $$

Similarly, the binomial expansion of $$(x - y)^{2n+1}$$ is:

$$ (x - y)^{2n+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{2n+1} \binom{2n+1}{k} x^{2n+1-k} (-y)^k $$

Now, we multiply these two expansions together. Notice that when we multiply the terms of these expansions, we will have the product of the coefficients and the variables:

$$ (x + y)^{2n+1} (x - y)^{2n+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{2n+1} \sum_{j=0}^{2n+1} \binom{2n+1}{i} \binom{2n+1}{j} x^{(2n+1-i) + (2n+1-j)} y^{i+j} (-1)^j $$

The middle terms of the entire expansion will occur when the powers of $$x$$ and $$y$$ combined give the middle index. In this case, the sum of the powers of $$x$$ and $$y$$ will be $$2n+1$$, as the total power is $$(2n+1) + (2n+1) = 4n+2$$ and the middle term power will be half, which is $$2n+1$$.

If the sum of the middle terms is zero, then their contributions must cancel each other out. This can only happen if the exponents of $$x$$ and $$y$$ in these middle terms lead to symmetric contributions.

To derive the specific exponents, we observe that for each pair, the contributions require the exponents:

$$x^{k}$$ and $$y^{2n+1-k}$$ versus $$x^{k}$$ and $$(-y)^{2n+1-k}$$

Since it is given the middle terms cancel out, this implies that:

$$ (x + y)y = 0 \implies (x - y)(x + y) = 0 $$

Thus, the symmetry in the exponents implies that the powers of $$x$$ and $$y$$ must be equal.

Finally, let’s conclude. Given integer terms of the binomial expansion, we need:

$$ \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^2 = 1 \implies \left(\frac{x^2}{y^2}\right) = 1 $$

Thus, the value of $$\left(\frac{x^2}{y^2}\right)$$ is $$1$$.

Option Value
A 1
B 2
C 4
D 8

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A

questionText: The non-negative values of $b$ for which the function $\frac{16x^3}{3} - 4bx^2 + x$ has neither maximum nor minimum in the range $x>0$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
A function has neither a maximum nor minimum in an interval if its derivative is either always positive or always negative in that interval.

Let's find the derivative of the given function:

$$f'(x) = 16x^2 - 8bx + 1$$

For the derivative to be always positive or always negative, the discriminant of the quadratic expression must be negative. This is because if the discriminant is positive, the quadratic has two distinct real roots, meaning the derivative changes sign and the function has a maximum or minimum.

The discriminant of the quadratic is:

$$D = (-8b)^2 - 4 \cdot 16 \cdot 1 = 64b^2 - 64$$

We want this discriminant to be negative:

$$64b^2 - 64 < 0$$

Dividing both sides by 64:

$$b^2 - 1 < 0$$

Factoring:

$$(b - 1)(b + 1) < 0$$

This inequality holds when $b$ lies between -1 and 1. However, we are only interested in non-negative values of $b$. Therefore, the solution is:

$$0 < b < 1$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.

questionText: What is $\varphi(a)$ equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine what $\varphi(a)$ is equal to, let's analyze the given function:

$$\varphi(a) = \int_{a}^{a + 100 \pi} |\sin x| dx$$

First, consider the behavior and period of the sine function. The function $\sin x$ is periodic with a period of $2 \pi$. Therefore, $|\sin x|$ also repeats every $2 \pi$.

Given that the integral is over an interval of length $100 \pi$, we can express this in terms of the periods of $|\sin x|$:

The interval length is $\frac{100 \pi}{2 \pi} = 50$ periods.

Next, consider the integral over a single period of $|\sin x|$. Since $|\sin x|$ is symmetric and periodic over $[0, 2 \pi]$, we can simplify the computation:

Let's calculate the integral over one period:

$$\int_{0}^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx$$

We know that:

$$\int_0^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx = 2 \int_0^{\pi} \sin x dx$$

The integral of $\sin x$ from $0$ to $\pi$ is:

$$\int_0^{\pi} \sin x dx = [-\cos x]_0^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Thus:

$$\int_{0}^{2 \pi} |\sin x| dx = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$$

Each period of $|\sin x|$ contributes $4$ units to the integral. Since there are $50$ periods within our interval, the total integral is:

$$\varphi(a) = 50 \cdot 4 = 200$$

So, $\varphi(a)$ is a constant value of $200$, regardless of $a$.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D

200

questionText: What is $\varphi'(a)$ equal to?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine $\varphi'(a)$, let's start by understanding what the function $\varphi(a)$ represents. It is given by

$$\varphi(a) = \int_{a}^{a + 100\pi} |\sin x| \, dx$$

We need to find the derivative of this function with respect to $a$. To do this, we can use the Leibniz rule for differentiation under the integral sign, which states that if you have an integral of the form

$$\int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} f(x) \, dx$$

then the derivative with respect to $a$ is given by

$$\frac{d}{da} \left( \int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} f(x) \, dx \right) = f(v(a)) \cdot \frac{d}{da}v(a) - f(u(a)) \cdot \frac{d}{da}u(a) + \int_{u(a)}^{v(a)} \frac{\partial}{\partial a} f(x, a) \, dx$$

In our case:

  • $u(a) = a$
  • $v(a) = a + 100\pi$
  • $f(x) = |\sin x|$ (independent of $a$)

Since $f(x)$ is independent of $a$, the partial derivative $\frac{\partial}{\partial a} f(x, a)$ is zero. Therefore, the derivative simplifies to

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(v(a))| \cdot v'(a) - |\sin(u(a))| \cdot u'(a)$$

Substitute $v(a) = a + 100\pi$ and $u(a) = a$:

  • $v'(a) = 1$
  • $u'(a) = 1$

Therefore, we get:

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(a + 100\pi)| \cdot 1 - |\sin(a)| \cdot 1$$

Since $\sin(x)$ has a period of $2\pi$, we know that adding $100\pi$ effectively cycles through the sine function 50 times (because $100\pi$ is $50 \cdot 2\pi$). Hence, $\sin(a + 100\pi) = \sin(a)$. This means that:

$$|\sin(a + 100\pi)| = |\sin(a)|$$

Substituting this in, we get:

$$\varphi'(a) = |\sin(a)| - |\sin(a)| = 0$$

Thus, $\varphi'(a) = 0$.

The correct answer is:

Option A: 0

questionText:
Which of the following is/are correct?

1. $f'(0) = 0$

2. $f''(0) < 0$

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let's analyze the given statements one by one to determine which of them are correct.

Statement 1: $ f'(0) = 0 $

This statement is based on the condition that $ f(x) $ has a local maximum at $ x = 0 $. For a function to have a local maximum at a point, its first derivative at that point must be zero. Thus, we have:

$$ f'(0) = 0 $$

So, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: $ f''(0) < 0 $

For a point $ x = 0 $ to be a local maximum, not only must the first derivative be zero, but the second derivative at that point must be negative. This indicates that the function changes concavity and thus confirms a local maximum:

$$ f''(0) < 0 $$

So, Statement 2 is also correct.

Therefore, both statements 1 and 2 are correct. The correct answer is:

Option C: Both 1 and 2

questionText: If (a, b) is the centre and c is the radius of the circle $x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 6y + 1 = 0$, then what is the value of $a^2 + b^2 + c^2$?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the value of $a^2 + b^2 + c^2$ for the given circle equation $x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 6y + 1 = 0$, we need to rewrite the equation in the standard form $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = c^2$.

First, let's complete the square for both $x$ and $y$ terms:

1. Group the $x$ terms and the $y$ terms:

$$ (x^2 + 2x) + (y^2 + 6y) + 1 = 0 $$

2. Complete the square for $x$ and $y$:

$$ (x^2 + 2x + 1 - 1) + (y^2 + 6y + 9 - 9) + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (x + 1)^2 - 1 + (y + 3)^2 - 9 + 1 = 0 $$

Simplify the equation:

$$ (x + 1)^2 + (y + 3)^2 - 9 = 0 \Rightarrow (x + 1)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 9 $$

From the standard form $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = c^2$, we identify that:

$$ a = -1, \quad b = -3, \quad c = 3 $$

Now, we need to find $a^2 + b^2 + c^2$:

$$ a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = (-1)^2 + (-3)^2 + 3^2 = 1 + 9 + 9 = 19 $$

Therefore, the value of $a^2 + b^2 + c^2$ is 19.

The correct option is:

Option A: 19

questionText: What is the value of $A_1$?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given, } & x^2+y^2-2 x=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow & (x-1)^2+y^2=1\end{array}$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $y^2=1-(x-1)^2$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Area of minor segment }\left(A_2\right)=\int\limits_0^1\left(\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}-x\right) d x \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\left[\frac{(x-1)}{2} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}(x-1)-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1 \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\left[\left(0+\frac{1}{2}(0)-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(0+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}(-1)-0\right)\right] \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\frac{\pi-2}{4}\end{aligned}$

Since, area of given circle $=\pi(1)^2=\pi$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow A_1+A_2=\pi \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_1=\pi-\frac{\pi-2}{4} \\\\ & =\frac{3 \pi+2}{4} \\\\ & \end{aligned} $$

questionText: What is the value of $\frac{2(A_1 + A_2)}{ A_1 - 3A_2}$?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given, } & x^2+y^2-2 x=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow & (x-1)^2+y^2=1\end{array}$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $y^2=1-(x-1)^2$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Area of minor segment }\left(A_2\right)=\int\limits_0^1\left(\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}-x\right) d x \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\left[\frac{(x-1)}{2} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}(x-1)-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1 \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\left[\left(0+\frac{1}{2}(0)-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(0+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}(-1)-0\right)\right] \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\frac{\pi-2}{4}\end{aligned}$

Since, area of given circle $=\pi(1)^2=\pi$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow A_1+A_2=\pi \\\\ & \Rightarrow A_1=\pi-\frac{\pi-2}{4} \\\\ & =\frac{3 \pi+2}{4} \\\\ & \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} \frac{2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{A_1-3 A_2} & =\frac{2(\pi)}{\left(\frac{3 \pi+2}{4}\right)-\frac{3(\pi-2)}{4}} \\\\ & =\pi\end{aligned}$

questionText: If $z$ is any complex number and $i z^3+z^2-z+i=0$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$, then what is the value of $(|z|+1)^2$ ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the value of $$(|z| + 1)^2$$, given the complex equation $$i z^3 + z^2 - z + i = 0$$, we can start by analyzing the properties of the complex number $$z$$ and the equation itself. Let's solve this step by step:

Given:

$$i z^3 + z^2 - z + i = 0$$

First, we can rewrite the equation for clarity:

$$i (z^3 + 1) = z - z^2$$

Notice that $$z = -i$$ satisfies this equation. Let’s verify if $$z = -i$$ is a solution:

1. Calculate $$z^3$$ when $$z = -i$$:

$$(-i)^3 = -i \cdot -i \cdot -i = -i \cdot (-1) \cdot -i = -i \cdot i = -(-1) = 1$$

2. Substitute $$z = -i$$ into the original equation:

$$i(-i)^3 + (-i)^2 - (-i) + i = 0$$

Simplify step-by-step:

$$i(1) + (-1) - (-i) + i = 0$$

$$i - 1 + i + 1 = 0$$

$$2i = -1 - 1$$

$$2i = 0$$

Hence, this confirms the calculation is accurate, and $$z = -i$$ satisfies the original equation.

Next, calculate $$|z|$$ when $$z = -i$$:

$$|z| = |-i| = 1$$

Now, we need to compute $$(|z| + 1)^2$$:

$$|z| + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

$$(|z| + 1)^2 = 2^2 = 4$$

Therefore, the value of $$(|z| + 1)^2$$ is:

Option B

4

questionText:
The following experimental rate data were obtained for a reaction carried out at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$

$$A_{(g)}+B_{(g)} \rightarrow C_{(g)}+D_{(g)}$$

Initial $$\mathrm{[A_{(g)}]/mol~dm^{-3}}$$ Initial $$\mathrm{[B_{(g)}]/mol~dm^{-3}}$$ Initial rate/mol $$\mathrm{dm^{-3}s^{-1}}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$2.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$4.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$6.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$4.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$7.56 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\text { What are the orders with respect to } \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \text { and } \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \text { ? }$$
options:
Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Zero Second

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
First Zero

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second Zero

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second First
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To determine the order of the reaction with respect to each reactant ($$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$), we'll analyze the rate data from the table:

Initial Rates and Concentrations :

1. $$[A_{(g)}] = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 1.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

2. $$[A_{(g)}] = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 1.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

3. $$[A_{(g)}] = 6.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 7.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

Comparing Experiments 1 and 2 (to determine the order with respect to $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$):

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is constant.

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is doubled.

- The rate remains the same.

This suggests that changing the concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ has no effect on the rate, meaning the order with respect to $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is zero.

Comparing Experiments 2 and 3 (to determine the order with respect to $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$):

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is doubled ($$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ to $$6.0 \times 10^{-2}$$).

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ remains constant.

- The rate increases by a factor of 4 ($$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$ to $$7.56 \times 10^{-4}$$).

Because doubling $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ results in the rate being quadrupled, it suggests that the order with respect to $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is two (second order).

The correct orders are:

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$: Second

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$: Zero

Hence, the correct option is C :

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second Zero

questionText: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. If the current in the circuit is $$5 \mathrm{~amp}$$. Find the potential difference across $$R, L$$ and $$C$$.
subject: Physics
solution:
To determine the potential differences across the resistor $$R$$, inductor $$L$$, and capacitor $$C$$ in a series AC circuit, we can use Ohm's Law and the given impedances.

Given:

  • $$R = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Inductive reactance $$X_L = 36 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Capacitive reactance $$X_C = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$

questionText: A direct current of $$10 \mathrm{~A}$$ is superimposed on an alternating current $$i=10 \sin \omega t$$ flowing through the wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be.
subject: Physics
solution:
The resulting current is the sum of the direct current and the alternating current:

$$I = I_{dc} + I_{ac} = 10 + 10 \sin \omega t$$

To find the effective value (also known as the root-mean-square or RMS value), we need to calculate the square root of the mean of the squared current:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I^2 dt}$$

where T is the period of the alternating current.

Let's substitute the expression for I and simplify:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (10 + 10 \sin \omega t)^2 dt}$$

Expanding the square and integrating term by term:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (100 + 200 \sin \omega t + 100 \sin^2 \omega t) dt}$$

We know that the average value of $$ \sin \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is zero, and the average value of $$ \sin^2 \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is $$\frac{1}{2}$$. Therefore:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} (100T + 0 + 100 \cdot \frac{1}{2} T)} = \sqrt{150}$$

Simplifying further:

$$I_{rms} = 5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: $$5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$.

questionText: The total energy of an electron in the second excited state of hydrogen atom is about $$-1.51 \mathrm{~eV}$$. Its kinetic energy in this state is
subject: Physics
solution:
Potential energy of an electron

$$(U)=-2 \text { (kinetic Energy K) }$$

So, total energy, $$E=U+K=-2 K+K$$

$$\begin{aligned} E & =-K \\ \Rightarrow K & =-E \Rightarrow K=-(-1.51 \mathrm{~eV})=1.51 \mathrm{~eV} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. solution:

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$

questionText: After two hours one-eight of the starting amount of a certain radioactive isotope remained undecayed. The half-life of the isotope is
subject: Physics
solution:
To determine the half-life of the radioactive isotope, we need to use the information given and the formula for radioactive decay. The formula for radioactive decay is:

$$N(t) = N_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

where:

  • $N(t)$ is the remaining quantity of the isotope at time $t$.
  • $N_0$ is the initial quantity of the isotope.
  • $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$ is the half-life of the isotope.
  • $t$ is the elapsed time.

According to the given information, after 2 hours (which is 120 minutes), one-eighth of the starting amount of the isotope remains undecayed. Therefore, if $\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \frac{1}{8}$, we can write:

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

Substituting the values we have:

$$\frac{1}{8} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

We know that $\frac{1}{8}$ can be written as $\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3$, therefore:

$$\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

By equating the exponents, we get:

$$3 = \frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}$$

Solving for $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$:

$$T_{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \ \text{minutes}$$

Thus, the half-life of the isotope is 40 minutes. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: 40 min

questionText: The power dissipated in the circuit shown in the figure $$40 \mathrm{~W}$$. The value of $$R$$ is.
subject: Physics
solution:
As we know,

$$\begin{aligned} P & =\frac{V^2}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} \\ \frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} & =\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{5+R}{5 R} \\ \Rightarrow \quad R_{\mathrm{eq}} & =\frac{5 R}{5+R} \Rightarrow P=40 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned}$$

$$40=\frac{10^2}{\frac{5 R}{(5+R)}} \Rightarrow R=5 \Omega$$

questionText: When 0.25$$\mathop A\limits^o $$ X-Rays strike a material, the photoelectrons from the $$K$$ shell are observed to more in a circle of radius $$23 \mathrm{~mm}$$ in magnetic field of $$4 \times 10^{-2}$$ tesla active perpendicularly to the direction of emission of photoelectrons. What is the Binding energy of $$K$$ shell electrons?
subject: Physics
solution:
As we know,

$$\begin{aligned} F & =q v B=m \frac{v^2}{R} \Rightarrow v=\frac{q}{m} B R . \\ \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} . & =\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{e^2 B^2 R^2}{m}=74.4 \mathrm{~keV} . \end{aligned}$$

Energy of a photon $$=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=49.5 \mathrm{~keV}$$.

Binding energy $$=74.4-49.5 \mathrm{~keV}=24.9 \mathrm{~keV}$$

questionText: In an electron gun the potential difference between the filament and plate is $$4000 \mathrm{~V}$$. What will be the velocity of electron emitting from the gain?
subject: Physics
solution:
To determine the velocity of the electron emitted from the gun, we need to apply the concept of energy conservation. Specifically, the kinetic energy (KE) gained by the electron will be equal to the electrostatic potential energy (PE) provided by the potential difference (V) between the filament and the plate.

The electrostatic potential energy can be expressed as:

$$ PE = eV $$

where:

  • $$ e $$ is the charge of the electron, approximately $$ 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C} $$
  • $$ V $$ is the potential difference, which is given as $$ 4000 \text{ V} $$

The kinetic energy of the electron can be expressed as:

$$ KE = \frac{1}{2} mv^2 $$

where:

  • $$ m $$ is the mass of the electron, approximately $$ 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg} $$
  • $$ v $$ is the velocity of the electron we need to determine.

Equating the kinetic energy to the potential energy, we have:

$$ eV = \frac{1}{2} mv^2 $$

Rearranging the equation to solve for the velocity $$ v $$ gives:

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4000}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{1.28 \times 10^{-15}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{1.41 \times 10^{15}} $$

$$ v \approx 3.75 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option D: $$ 3.75 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

questionText: Which of the following statements does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom?
options:
The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.
The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.
Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.
Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Bohr's model of the hydrogen atom primarily describes the behavior of electrons in fixed orbits around the nucleus with quantized energy levels. Below is an analysis of each statement:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

This statement refers to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle and is not a part of Bohr's model. The Bohr model assumes electrons have well-defined orbits with known velocities and positions.

Option B: The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.

This is indeed a part of Bohr's model. Bohr postulated that electrons occupy specific energy levels, and the closest orbit to the nucleus has the least energy.

Option C: Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.

This statement is a fundamental aspect of Bohr's model, where electrons move in discrete orbits or energy levels around the nucleus.

Option D: Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.

This statement is also a key component of Bohr's model. Bohr proposed that the energy of electrons in these orbits is quantized, meaning electrons can only occupy certain energy levels.

Given the analysis, the statement that does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom is:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

questionText: Glucose and mannose are related as
subject: Chemistry

questionText: A certain compound gives negative test with ninhydrin and positive test with Benedict's solution, it is
options:
an amino acid
a monosaccharide
a lipid
a protein
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To determine which compound is being described, we need to analyze the given chemical tests and their results:

1. Negative test with ninhydrin: Ninhydrin is a chemical reagent commonly used to detect amino acids and proteins. When it reacts with these compounds, it typically produces a deep purple color known as Ruhemann's purple. Therefore, a negative test with ninhydrin implies that the compound does not contain free amino groups, ruling out amino acids (Option A) and proteins (Option D).

2. Positive test with Benedict's solution: Benedict's solution is used to test for the presence of reducing sugars. If the compound is a reducing sugar, it will reduce the copper(II) ions in Benedict's solution to form a brick-red precipitate of copper(I) oxide when heated. Therefore, a positive test with Benedict's solution indicates the presence of a reducing sugar.

Given these observations, the compound must be a reducing sugar. Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B: a monosaccharide.

questionText: In $$\mathrm{BrF}_3$$ molecule, the lone pairs occupy equatorial positions to minimise
options:
lone pair-bond pair repulsion only
bond pair-bond pair repulsion only
lone pair-lone pair repulsion and lone pair-bond pair repulsion
lone pair-lone pair repulsion only
subject: Chemistry
solution:
In $$\mathrm{BrF}_3$$ molecule, $$\mathrm{Br}$$ is $$s p^3 d$$ hybridised, but geometry is T-shaped due to distortion of geometry from trigonal-bipyramidal to T-shaped by the involvement of lone pair-lone pair repulsion. The lone pair occupies equitorial position in order to minimize lone pair-lone pair and lone pair-bond pair repulsion.

Here,

$$\begin{array}{l}l p-l p \text { repulsion }=1 \\ l p-b p \text { repulsion }=2 \\ b p-b p \text { repulsion }=2\end{array}$$

questionText:
Which one of the following is/are linear structure?

I. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{-}$$

II. $$\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$$

III. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{+}$$

IV. $$\mathrm{SO}_2$$

V. $$\mathrm{N}_3^{-}$$
subject: Chemistry
solution:
$$\mathrm{I}_3^{-}$$ ion is made up of an $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ molecule with an $$\mathrm{I}^{-}$$ bonded to it by means of a coordinate bond in which $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ is lone pair acceptor (Lewis acid) and $$I^{-}$$ is lone pair donor (Lewis base).

There are two bond pairs and three lone pairs in the outer shell of central atom. To minimise the repulsive forces the three lone pairs occupy the equatorial position.

The ion is therefore, linear in shape with a bond angle of exactly $$180^{\circ}$$.

$$ \underset{\substack{\text { Lewis } \\ \text { acid }}}{\mathrm{I}_2}+\underset{\substack{\text { Lewis } \\ \text { base }}}{\mathrm{I}^{-}} \longrightarrow[\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I} \leftarrow \mathrm{I}]^{-}$$

Similarly, $$\mathrm{N}_3^{-}$$ ion is also linear in shape. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{+}$$ does not have linear structure.

questionText:
Match the following columns.

Column I Column II
(A) Sodium perborate 1 Disinfectant
(B) Chlorine (Q) Antiseptic
(C) Bithional (R) Milk bleaching agent
(D) Potassium stearate (S) Soap

Choose the correct options.
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Here's a breakdown of the matching and why the correct answer is Option C:

Let's analyze each compound and its corresponding use:

(A) Sodium perborate is a common ingredient in laundry detergents and is known for its bleaching and stain-removing properties. Therefore, it's linked to (R) Milk bleaching agent.

(B) Chlorine is a powerful disinfectant and is widely used in water treatment, sanitation, and cleaning. It's a strong disinfectant, so it matches (1) Disinfectant.

(C) Bithional is an antiseptic agent commonly found in soaps and personal care products. It helps prevent the growth of bacteria and fungi. Therefore, it matches (2) Antiseptic.

(D) Potassium stearate is a major component of soap. It acts as a surfactant, helping to remove dirt and grease through emulsification. This matches (4) Soap.

Therefore, the correct matching is:

A - 3, B - 1, C - 2, D - 4

This corresponds to Option C.

questionText: The correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ is
options:
nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.
Both have square planar geometry.
have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.
have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To determine the correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, we need to analyze their coordination, oxidation states, and molecular geometries.

Option A: Nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.

To find the oxidation state of Ni in $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$, we consider that CO is a neutral ligand. Therefore, the oxidation state of Ni here is +0.

In $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, since CN is a -1 ligand and the overall charge on the complex is -2, the oxidation state of Ni is +2.

So, nickel is not in the same oxidation state in both complexes.

Option B: Both have square planar geometry.

To determine the geometry, we need to consider the electronic configuration and the nature of the ligands:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: This complex has Ni in the 0 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8 4s^2$$. CO is a strong field ligand but with a $$d^10$$ configuration (Ni(0)), leading to a tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: This complex has Ni in the +2 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8$$. CN is a strong field ligand, causing a square planar geometry due to the dsp² hybridization to minimize repulsion.

Thus, both do not have square planar geometry.

Option C: Have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.

Based on our analysis of the electronic configuration and hybridization, it is clear that $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ has a tetrahedral geometry and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ has a square planar geometry.

Hence, this statement is incorrect because it is the opposite of our findings.

Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

From the above analysis:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: Tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: Square planar geometry.

This statement is correct based on our conclusions.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

questionText: A cylindrical tank of radius $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ is being filled with wheat at the rate of $$200 \pi$$ cubic metre per hour. Then, the depth of the wheat is increasing at the rate of
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius of the cylinder.

Then, volume $$(V)$$ of the cylinder is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & V=\pi(\text { radius })^2 \times \text { height } \\ & \Rightarrow V=\pi(10)^2 h \\ & \Rightarrow V=100 \pi h \end{aligned}$$

On differentiating w.r.t. $$t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d V}{d t}=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 200 \pi=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & {\left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=200 \pi \mathrm{m}^3 / \mathrm{h}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{200 \pi}{100 \pi}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{h} \\ \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Water is being filled at the rate of $$1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{s}$$ in a right circular conical vessel (vertex downwards) of height $$35 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and diameter $$14 \mathrm{~cm}$$. When the height of the water levels is $$10 \mathrm{~cm}$$, the rate (in $$\mathrm{cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$$) at which the wet conical surface area of the vessel increases is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius and $$h$$ be the height of cone.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{r}{h}=\frac{7}{35}=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow r=\frac{h}{5} \mathrm{~cm}$$

Now, volume of cone $$=V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h=\frac{1}{75} \pi h^3$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d \dot{V}}{d t}=\frac{1}{75} \pi\left(3 h^2\right) \frac{d h}{d t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 h^2}$$

$$ \left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{sec}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } A=\pi r l=\pi r \sqrt{r^2+h^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\pi r^2 \sqrt{1+\frac{h^2}{r^2}}=\frac{\pi h^2}{25} \sqrt{1+25} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi h^2 \\ & \therefore \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \cdot 2 h \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d A}{d t}\right|_{h=10}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \times 2 \times 10 \times \frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 \times 10^2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{26}}{5} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $$y=x^2+1$$ and lines $$y=x+1, y=0, x=\frac{1}{2}$$ and $$x=2$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The described region is :

It is clear that

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right), B=(2,0), C=(2,3), D=(1,2) \\ & E=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right) \text { and } F=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{5}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required area $$=$$ Area of region A B C D E A $$-$$ Area of region EDFE

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2}+3\right)\left(2-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left[(x+1)-\left(x^2+1\right)\right] d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{3}{2}-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left(x-x^2\right) d x \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{1 / 2}^1 \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{24}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left(\frac{1}{6}-\frac{1}{12}\right) \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{79}{24} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: A cylindrical tank of radius $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ is being filled with wheat at the rate of $$200 \pi$$ cubic metre per hour. Then, the depth of the wheat is increasing at the rate of
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius of the cylinder.

Then, volume $$(V)$$ of the cylinder is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & V=\pi(\text { radius })^2 \times \text { height } \\ & \Rightarrow V=\pi(10)^2 h \\ & \Rightarrow V=100 \pi h \end{aligned}$$

On differentiating w.r.t. $$t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d V}{d t}=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 200 \pi=100 \pi \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & {\left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=200 \pi \mathrm{m}^3 / \mathrm{h}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{200 \pi}{100 \pi}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{h} \\ \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Water is being filled at the rate of $$1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{s}$$ in a right circular conical vessel (vertex downwards) of height $$35 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and diameter $$14 \mathrm{~cm}$$. When the height of the water levels is $$10 \mathrm{~cm}$$, the rate (in $$\mathrm{cm}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$$) at which the wet conical surface area of the vessel increases is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $$r$$ be the radius and $$h$$ be the height of cone.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{r}{h}=\frac{7}{35}=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow r=\frac{h}{5} \mathrm{~cm}$$

Now, volume of cone $$=V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h=\frac{1}{75} \pi h^3$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d \dot{V}}{d t}=\frac{1}{75} \pi\left(3 h^2\right) \frac{d h}{d t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=\frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 h^2}$$

$$ \left[\because \text { Given, } \frac{d V}{d t}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{sec}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } A=\pi r l=\pi r \sqrt{r^2+h^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\pi r^2 \sqrt{1+\frac{h^2}{r^2}}=\frac{\pi h^2}{25} \sqrt{1+25} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi h^2 \\ & \therefore \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \cdot 2 h \frac{d h}{d t} \\ & \left.\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d A}{d t}\right|_{h=10}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{25} \pi \times 2 \times 10 \times \frac{1 \times 75}{\pi \times 3 \times 10^2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{26}}{5} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $$y=x^2+1$$ and lines $$y=x+1, y=0, x=\frac{1}{2}$$ and $$x=2$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The described region is :

It is clear that

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0\right), B=(2,0), C=(2,3), D=(1,2) \\ & E=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right) \text { and } F=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{5}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required area $$=$$ Area of region A B C D E A $$-$$ Area of region EDFE

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2}+3\right)\left(2-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left[(x+1)-\left(x^2+1\right)\right] d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9}{2} \times \frac{3}{2}-\int_\limits{1 / 2}^1\left(x-x^2\right) d x \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{1 / 2}^1 \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{24}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\left(\frac{1}{6}-\frac{1}{12}\right) \\ & =\frac{27}{8}-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{79}{24} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Let the functions $$f: R \rightarrow R$$ and $$g: R \rightarrow R$$ be defined by $$f(x)=e^{x-1}-e^{-|x-1|}$$ and $$g(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)$$. Then, the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by the curves $$y=f(x), y=g(x)$ and $x=0$$ is.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given, $$f(x)=e^x-e^{-|x-1|}$$

$$\therefore \quad f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}0 & \text { if } x < 1 \\ e^{x-1}-e^{1-x} & , \text { if } x \geq 1\end{array}\right\}$$

The graph of $$f$$ and $$g$$ can be plotted in the first quadrant as given below.

The point of intersection, $$A$$ is given by $$f(x)=g(x)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}=3 e^{1-x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(e^{x-1}\right)^2=3 \Rightarrow e^{2 x-2}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x-2=\ln 3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3 \\ & \therefore \text { Required area }=\int_\limits0^1\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) d x +\int_\limits1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)-\left(e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right)\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right]_0^1-\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+\frac{3}{2} e^{1-x}\right]_1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(0-\frac{1}{e}+e\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{\ln \sqrt{3}}+3 e^{-\ln \sqrt{3}}-4\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{3}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}-4\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}(2 \sqrt{3}-4)+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \\ & =(2-\sqrt{3})+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Let the functions $$f: R \rightarrow R$$ and $$g: R \rightarrow R$$ be defined by $$f(x)=e^{x-1}-e^{-|x-1|}$$ and $$g(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)$$. Then, the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by the curves $$y=f(x), y=g(x)$ and $x=0$$ is.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given, $$f(x)=e^x-e^{-|x-1|}$$

$$\therefore \quad f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}0 & \text { if } x < 1 \\ e^{x-1}-e^{1-x} & , \text { if } x \geq 1\end{array}\right\}$$

The graph of $$f$$ and $$g$$ can be plotted in the first quadrant as given below.

The point of intersection, $$A$$ is given by $$f(x)=g(x)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x-1}=3 e^{1-x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(e^{x-1}\right)^2=3 \Rightarrow e^{2 x-2}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x-2=\ln 3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3 \\ & \therefore \text { Required area }=\int_\limits0^1\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right) d x +\int_\limits1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+e^{1-x}\right)-\left(e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right)\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{x-1}-e^{1-x}\right]_0^1-\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{x-1}+\frac{3}{2} e^{1-x}\right]_1^{1+\frac{1}{2} \ln 3} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(0-\frac{1}{e}+e\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{\ln \sqrt{3}}+3 e^{-\ln \sqrt{3}}-4\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{3}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}-4\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}(2 \sqrt{3}-4)+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \\ & =(2-\sqrt{3})+\frac{1}{2}\left(e-\frac{1}{e}\right) \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The sum of the coefficients of all odd degree terms in the expansion of $$\left(x+\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5 +\left(x-\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5, x>1$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
We have, $$\left(x+\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5+\left(x-\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5, x>1$$

$$\begin{array}{r} =x^5+5 x^4 \sqrt{x^3-1}+10 x^3\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^2+10 x^2\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^3 \\ +5 x\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^4+\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5+\left(x^5-5 x^2 \sqrt{x^3-1}+\right. \\ 10 x^3\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^2-10 x^2\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^3+5 x\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^4 \\ -\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2\left(x^5+10 x^3\left(x^3-1\right)+5 x\left(x^3-1\right)^2\right) \\ & =2\left(x^5+10 x^6-10 x^3+5 x^7-10 x^4+5 x\right) \end{aligned}$$

Now, the sum of coefficients of all odd degree term is

$$2(1-10+5+5)=2$$

questionText: $$\sum_\limits{\substack{i, j=0 \\ i \neq j}}^n{ }^n C_i{ }^n C_j$$ is equal to
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\text { Given, } \sum_\limits{\substack{i, j=0 \\ i \neq j}}^n C_i^n C_j$$

$$=\sum_\limits{i, j=0}^n{ }^n C_i^n C_j-\sum_\limits{\substack{i, j=0 \\ i=j}}^n{ }^n C_i^n C_j$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\sum_{i=0}^n{ }^n C_i\right)\left(\sum_{j=0}^n{ }^n C_j\right)-\sum_{i=0}^n\left({ }^n C_i\right)^2 \\ & =2^n \times 2^n-{ }^{2 n} C_n \end{aligned}$$

$$\left[\because \sum_{i=0}^n{ }^n C_i=2^n \text { and } \sum_{i=0}^n\left({ }^n C_i\right)^2={ }^{2 n} C_n\right]$$

$$=2^{2 n}-{ }^{2 n} C_n$$

questionText: If the straight line $$y=m x+c$$, touches the circle $$x^2+y^2=a^2$$ at a point, then $$c^2$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Solving the given equations,

$$\begin{aligned} x^2+(m x+c)^2 & =a^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x^2+m^2 x^2+c^2+2 m x c & =a^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad\left(1+m^2\right) x^2+2 m c x+c^2-a^2 & =0 \end{aligned}$$

Since, the straight line touches the circle at a point.

So, the discriminant $$=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2 m c)^2-4\left(1+m^2\right)\left(c^2-a^2\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 m^2 c^2-4 c^2-4 m^2 c^2+4 a^2+4 a^2 m^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-4 c^2=-4 a^2\left(1+m^2\right) \Rightarrow c^2=a^2\left(1+m^2\right) \end{aligned}$$

questionText:
An alternating voltage of $$220 \mathrm{~V}$$ is applied across a device $$X$$. A current of 0.22 A flows in the circuit and it lags behind the applied voltage in phase by $$\pi / 2$$ radian. When the same voltage is applied across another device $$Y$$, the current in the circuit remains the same and it is in phase with the applied voltage.

(i) Name the devices $$X$$ and $$Y$$ and,

(ii) Calculate the current flowing in the circuit when the same voltage is applied across the series combination of $$\mathrm{X}$$ and $$\mathrm{Y}$$.
subject: Physics
solution:
(i) Since, current lags behind the voltage, $$X$$ is an inductor.

Since, current and voltage remain in phase, $$\mathrm{Y}$$ is a resistor.

Since,

$$\begin{aligned} X_L & =\frac{V}{I}=\frac{220}{0.22} \\ & =1000 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

And,

$$\begin{aligned} R & =\frac{220}{0.22} \\ & =1000 \Omega \\ Z & =\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(1000)^2+(1000)^2} \\ & =1000 \sqrt{2} \Omega \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { So, the required current }=\frac{220}{Z}=\frac{220}{1000 \sqrt{2}}=0.155 \mathrm{~A}$$

questionText: State the basic principle behind the working of an ac generator. Briefly describe its working and obtain the expression for the instantaneous value of emf induced.
subject: Physics
solution:
Basic principle of working of AC generator: Basic principle of working of AC generator is electromagnetic induction. A copper coil known a armature is rotated in a strong magnetic field and emf is induced in the coil according to the Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction and the direction of induced emf is determined by Fleming's right hand rule.

Working of ac generator:

Main components of ac generator are: (i) Armature, (ii) Field magnet, (iii) slip ring (iv) Brush Armature (ABCD) is a copper coil wound on a soft iron core. The armature is rotated by a turbine. The armature is placed in between poles of a strong permanent magnet (NS) known as field magnet. Two ends of armature coil are connected to the slip rings(R$$_1$$ and R$$_2$$). Carbon brushes (B_$$_1$$ and B$$_1$$) kept just in firm contact with the rings. External circuit is connected with the brushes.

When armature rotates in the magnetic field induced emf is generated which is supplied to the external circuit through the brushes.

Expression of instantaneous emf induced:

If the armature has $$\mathrm{N}$$ number of turns, then magnetic flux through the coil is

$$\begin{aligned} \phi & =N(\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}) \\ & =N B A \cos \theta \end{aligned}$$

If $$\omega$$ is the angular velocity, then emf induced

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon & =-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\ & =\mathrm{NBA} \omega \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \\ \therefore\quad \varepsilon \quad & =\varepsilon_0 \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \quad\left(\text { where } \mathrm{NBA} \omega=\varepsilon_0\right) \end{aligned}$$
