questionText:
(a) (i) Differentiate between 'distance of closest approach' and 'impact parameter'.

(ii) Determine the distance of closest approach when an alpha particle of kinetic energy 3.95 $$\mathrm{MeV}$$ approaches a nucleus of $$Z=79$$, stops and reverses its directions.

OR

(b) (i) State three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom.

(ii) Find the angular momentum of an electron revolving in the second orbit in Bohr's hydrogen atom.
subject: Physics
solution:
(a) (i) Difference between "distance of closest approach" and "impact parameter":

Distance of closest approach Impact Parameter
Distance of closest approach is the distance of a charged particle from the centre of the nucleus where the total kinetic energy of the charged particle gets converted into potential energy. Impact parameter is the perpendicular distance between the path of projected charged particles and centre of the nucleus.
$$r=\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)}$$ $$b=\frac{Z e^2 \cot (\theta / 2)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)}$$

$$\text { (ii) Distance of closest approach }=r=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 E}$$

$$\begin{aligned} Q & =79 \mathrm{e}=79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ q & =2 \mathrm{e}=2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ E & =3.95 \mathrm{MeV} \\ & =3.95 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore r=\left(9 \times 10^9\right) \frac{\left(79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{3.95 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =576 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =5.76 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) (i) Postulates of Bohr's theory: Postulate 1: In an atom electrons are revolving around the nucleus in definite circular orbits. These orbits are called 'stationary orbits' and each orbit or shell possesses fixed energy. While revolving in these orbits electrons do not emit any radiation.

Postulate 2: Electrons can move only those permissible orbits where the angular momenta of electrons are integral multiples of $$\frac{h}{2 \pi}$$ where $$h$$ is the Planck's constant.

Postulate 3: Transition of electrons may occur from one stationary orbit to another. During such transition energy may be emitted or absorbed following the relation $$E_i-E_f=h v$$ (where $$E_i \sim E_f$$ is the difference of energies of the two stable orbits).

(ii) Angular momentum of electron revolving in $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ orbit in Bohr's Hydrogen atom:

Angular momentum $$=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$$

Here, $$n=2$$,

$$\therefore$$ Angular momentum $$=2 \times \frac{h}{2 \pi}=\frac{h}{\pi}$$

questionText: The radius of the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit in Bohr model of hydrogen atom is proportional
subject: Physics
solution: $$r_n \propto n^2 / \mathrm{Z}$$

questionText: The capacitors, each of $$4 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ are to be connected in such a way that the effective capacitance of the combination is $$6 \mu \mathrm{F}$$. This can be achieved by connecting
options:
All three in parallel
All three in series
Two of them connected in series and the combination in parallel to the third.
Two of them connected in parallel and the combination in series to the third.
subject: Physics
solution: When two $$4 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ capacitors are joined in series, the equivalent capacitance becomes $$2 \mu \mathrm{F}$$. When this combination is joined in parallel with another $$4 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ capacitor, the effective capacitance becomes $$6 \mu \mathrm{F}$$.

questionText:
Assertion (A) : The equivalent resistance between points A and B in the given network is 2R.

Reason (R) : All the resistors are connected in parallel
options:
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A).
Assertion (A) is true and Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.
subject: Physics
solution: The given resistance network represents in the bridge balance condition, so the equivalent resistance between A and B will be 2R, Hence assertion (A) is correct but Reason (R) is false because all resistances are not in parallel.

questionText: The energy of a photon of wavelength $$\lambda%$ is
options:
$$h c \lambda$$
$$h c / \lambda$$
$$\lambda / h c$$
$$\lambda h / c$$
subject: Physics
solution:
Energy of photon,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}=h v \\ \text{But} \quad & \mathrm{v}=\frac{c}{\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

So, $$\mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

questionText: A hydrogen atom makes a transition from $$n=5$$ to $$n=1$$ orbit. The wavelength of photon emitted is $$\lambda$$. The wavelength of photon emitted when it makes a transition from $$n=5$$ to $$n=2$$ orbit is
subject: Physics
solution:
In 1st case

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{5^2}\right] \\ \text{or,} \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[1-\frac{1}{25}\right]=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{24}{25}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{R}=\frac{25}{24} \times \frac{1}{\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

In 2nd case

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{5^2}\right] \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{25}\right]=\mathrm{R} \times \frac{21}{100} \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\frac{25}{24} \times \frac{1}{\lambda} \times \frac{21}{100} \\ \text { or, } & \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}} =\frac{21}{96 \lambda} \\ \therefore & \lambda^{\prime} =\frac{96}{21} \lambda=\frac{32}{7} \lambda \end{array}$$

questionText: The curve of binding energy per nucleon as a function of atomic mass number has a sharp peak for helium nucleus. This implies that helium nucleus is
options:
radioactive
unstable
easily fissionable
more stable nucleus than its neighbours
subject: Physics
solution: A nucleus becomes stable when its binding energy per nucleon is around $$8 \mathrm{~MeV}$$. The peak indicates the binding energy per nucleon of He element is closer to $$8 \mathrm{~MeV}$$. Binding energy per nucleon of He in actually 7.1 MeV. So, it is stable than its neighbours.

questionText: A current of 0.8 A flows in a conductor of $$40 \Omega$$ for 1 minute. The heat produced in the conductor will be
subject: Physics
solution:
Given, $$R=40 \Omega \text { and } I=0.8 \mathrm{~A}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & t=1 \text { minute }=60 \mathrm{~s} \\ & \text { Heat produced }=H=I^2 R t \\ & \text { Or, } \quad H=(0.8)^2 \times 40 \times 60 \\ & \therefore \quad H=1536 \text { Joule } \\ & \end{aligned}$$

questionText: A series CR circuit with $$R=200 \Omega$$ and $$C=(50 / \pi)$$ $$\mu \mathrm{F}$$ is connected across an ac source of peak voltage $$\varepsilon_0=100 \mathrm{~V}$$ and frequency $$v=50 \mathrm{~Hz}$$. Calculate (a) impedance of the circuit $$(\mathrm{Z})$$, (b) phase angle $$(\phi)$$, and (c) voltage across the resistor.
subject: Physics
solution:
$$\text { (a) Given, } R=200 \Omega, C=\frac{50}{\pi} \mu F$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Impedance: } & Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2} \\ \text { Or, } & Z=\sqrt{(200)^2+\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{50}{\pi} \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2} \\ \text { Or, } & Z=\sqrt{(200)^2+(200)^2} \\ \therefore & Z=200 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$$

(b) Phase angle:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta & =\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\omega C R} \\ \text { or, } \quad \theta & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{50}{\pi} \times 10^{-6} \times 200}\right) \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\theta=\tan ^{-1} 1$$

$$\therefore \quad \theta=45^0$$

(c) Voltage across resistor:

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & I_{R M S} =\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{RMS}}}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ \text { Or, } & I_{R M S} =\frac{\frac{\varepsilon_0}{\sqrt{2}}}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ \text { Or, } & I_{R M S} =\frac{\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}}{200 \sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore & I_{R M S} =\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_R =I_{R M S} \times R \\ \text { Or, } & V_R =\frac{1}{4} \times 200 \\ \therefore & \quad V_R =50 \mathrm{~V} \end{array}$$

questionText:
(a) What is meant by ionisation energy? Write its value for hydrogen atom.

OR

(b) Define the term, mass defect. How is it related to stability of the nucleus?
subject: Physics
solution:
(a) Ionisation energy:

Ionisation energy is the minimum amount of energy which is to be supplied to an atom in its ground state so that it gets converted into an ion i.e., the minimum energy requires to shift an electron from $$n=1$$ to $$n=\infty$$.

The value of ionization energy for Hydrogen is 13.6 $$\mathrm{eV}$$.

OR

(b) Mass defect:

Mass defect is the difference of total mass of all the nucleons present in a nucleus and the rest mass of the nucleus.

Mass defect $$=\Delta m=Z m_P+(A-Z) m_n-M$$

As the mass defect increases, binding energy of the nucleus increases. This means the stability of the nucleus increases as mass defect increases.

questionText:
(a) (i) Distinguish between nuclear fission and fusion giving an example of each.

(ii) Explain the release of energy in nuclear fission and fusion on the basis of binding energy per nucleon curve.

OR

(b) (i) How is the size of a nucleus found experimentally? Write the relation between the radius and mass number of a nucleus.

(ii) Prove that the density of a nucleus is independent of its mass number.
subject: Physics
solution:
(a) (i) Nuclear fission & fusion:

Nuclear fission Nuclear fusion
Heavy nucleus splits into comparatively lighter nuclei. Lighter nuclei fuse to form comparatively heavier nuclei.
Energy released is less than that of fusion. Energy released is more than that of fission.
Does not naturally occur. Occur naturally in stars.
Comparatively less amount of energy required to start the process. Huge amount of energy is required to start the process.
Application: Atomic bomb Application: Hydrogen bomb
Example:
$$\begin{aligned}
& { }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}+{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+ { }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1+\mathrm{Q}
\end{aligned}$$		 Example:
$$\begin{aligned}
& { }_1 \mathrm{H}^1+{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^1 \rightarrow{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2+{ }_{+1} \mathrm{e}^0 +v+\mathrm{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

(ii) In nuclear fusion, the binding energy of the products is greater than the binding energy of reactants.

In nuclear fission, binding energy of fragments is greater than the binding energy of the parent.

This difference in binding energy is released in form of energy.

OR

(b) (i) Experimental determination of size of nucleus :

Size of nucleus was determined experimentally by Rutherford by his alpha particle scattering experiment.

In the experiment, a gold foil of thickness $$2.1 \times 10^{-7}$$ was bombarded by energetic $$\alpha$$-particles generated from $${ }_{83} \mathrm{Bi}^{214}$$ source.

Scattered $$\alpha$$-particles were observed on a $$\mathrm{ZnS}$$ screen with the help of a microscope.

It was observed that most of the $$\alpha$$-particles passed through the gold foil undeviated.

About $$14 \% ~\alpha$$-particles were scattered by an angle more than $$1^0$$.

1 out of $$8000 ~\alpha$$-particles was scattered by an angle more than $$90^{\circ}$$.

Very few $$\alpha$$-particles was scattered by an angle $$180^{\circ}$$.

From these observations Rutherford calculated the impact parameter and distance of closest approach and concluded that the size of nucleus lies between $$10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$$ and $$10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$$.

Relation between radius and mass number: $$\left.r=\mathrm{R}_0 \mathrm{A}^{1 / 3} \text { (where } R_0=1.25 \mathrm{fm}\right)$$

(ii) Density of nucleus is independent of mass number :

$$\begin{aligned} & \rho=\frac{M}{V} \\ \text{or,} \quad & \rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3} \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A} \quad\left(\text { since } r=R_0 A^{1 / 3}\right)$$

$$\therefore \quad \rho=\frac{m}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3}$$

Hence, it is independent of mass number.

questionText:
(a) Two charged conducting spheres of radii $$a$$ and $$b$$ are connected to each other by a wire. Find the ratio of the electric fields at their surfaces.

OR

(b) A parallel plate capacitor (A) of capacitance C is charged by a battery to voltage $$V$$. The battery is disconnected and an uncharged capacitor (B) of capacitance $$2 \mathrm{C}$$ is connected across $$\mathrm{A}$$. Find the ratio of

(i) final charges on A and B.

(ii) total electrostatic energy stored in A and B finally and that stored in A initially.
subject: Physics
solution:
(a) For sphere with radius a,

Potential $$=$$ V (since, both the charged spheres are connected by a wire)

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Charge } & =Q_a \\ \text { Capacitance } & =C_a \\ \text { Electric field, } & =E_a=\frac{Q_a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a^2} \end{aligned}$$

For sphere with radius $$b$$, Potential $$=V$$ (since both the charged spheres are connected by a wire)

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Charge } & =Q_b \\ \text { Capacitance } & =C_b \\ \text { Electric field } & =E_b \\ & =\frac{Q_b}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 b^2} \end{aligned}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{E_a}{E_b}=\frac{Q_a}{Q_b} \times \frac{b^2}{a^2} \quad \text{..... (1)}\\ & \frac{Q_a}{Q_b}=\frac{C_a V}{C_b V} \\ \text{Or,} \quad & \frac{Q_a}{Q_b}=\frac{C_a}{C_b} \\ \text{Or,} \quad & \frac{Q_a}{Q_b}=\frac{a}{b} \quad \text{.... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { (since, } C_d / C_b=a / b \text { ) }$$

Putting in equation (1)

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{E_a}{E_b}=\left(\frac{a}{b}\right) \times\left(\frac{b^2}{a^2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{E_a}{E_b}=\frac{b}{a} \\ \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) (i) Initially,

Charge on A capacitor $$=\mathrm{CV}$$

Charge on $$\mathrm{B}$$ capacitor $$=0$$

After connecting A with B,

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Final potential }=V^{\prime} & =\frac{\text { Total Charge }}{\text { total capacitance }} \\ & =\frac{Q+0}{C+2 C}=\frac{Q}{3 C} \end{aligned}$$

Final charge on A capacitor after redistribution

$$=C V^{\prime}=\frac{C Q}{3 C}=\frac{Q}{3}$$

Final charge on $$B$$ capacitor after redistribution

$$=2 C V^{\prime}=\frac{2 C Q}{3 C}=\frac{2 Q}{3}$$

So, the ratio of charges $$=\frac{Q / 3}{2 Q / 3}=1: 2$$

(ii) Initially energy stored in $$\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

Finally energy stored in $$\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{\prime 2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \times C \times\left(\frac{Q}{3 C}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times C \times\left(\frac{V}{3}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$=\frac{C V^2}{18}$$

Finally energy stored in $$\mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{2} 2 \mathrm{CV}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 C \times\left(\frac{Q}{3 C}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 C \times\left(\frac{V}{3}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{C V^2}{9} \end{aligned}$$

Total energy stored in A and B

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{C V^2}{18}+\frac{C V^2}{9} \\ & =\frac{C V^2}{6} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { So, the required ratio } & =\frac{\text { Find energy stored in } \mathrm{A} \text { and } \mathrm{B}}{\text { Initial energy stored in } \mathrm{A}} \\ & =\frac{C V^2 / 6}{C V^2 / 2} \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: A cell of emf $$E$$ is connected across an external resistance $$R$$. When current '$$I$$' is drawn from the cell, the potential difference across the electrodes of the cell drops to $$\mathrm{V}$$. The internal resistance '$$r$$' of the cell is
subject: Physics
solution:
Since, $$\mathrm{I}=\frac{E}{R+r}$$

So, $$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{I} r+\mathrm{V}$$

$$\therefore \quad r=\frac{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}$$

Or, $$r=\left(\frac{E-V}{V}\right) R \quad[\because \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{IR}]$$

questionText: Which one of the following metals does not exhibit emission of electrons from its surface when irradiated by visible light?
subject: Physics
solution: Cadmium emits electrons when irradiated by visible light.

questionText: $$\vec{E}$$ and $$\vec{B}$$ represent the electric and the magnetic field of an electro- magnetic wave respectively. The direction of propagation of the wave is along
subject: Physics
solution:
The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is given by the cross product of the electric field vector ($$\vec{E}$$) and the magnetic field vector ($$\vec{B}$$). Mathematically, this is represented as :

$$ \vec{E} \times \vec{B} $$

Hence, the direction of propagation of the wave is along Option C : $$\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$$.

In an electromagnetic wave, the electric field ($$\vec{E}$$), the magnetic field ($$\vec{B}$$), and the direction of propagation are all mutually perpendicular to each other. This relationship ensures that the wave propagates in a direction that is orthogonal to both the electric and magnetic fields.

To summarize, for an electromagnetic wave :

$$\vec{E} \perp \vec{B}$$

The wave propagation direction is given by $$\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$$

Thus, the correct answer is :

Option C

$$ \vec{E} \times \vec{B} $$

questionText: In an extrinsic semiconductor, the number density of holes is $$4 \times 10^{20} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$$. If the number density of intrinsic carriers is $$1.2 \times 10^{15} \mathbf{m}^3$$, the number density of electrons in it is
subject: Physics
solution:
From law of mass action,

$$\begin{aligned} n_i^2 & =n_e \times n_h \\ \text{or,} \quad \left(1.2 \times 10^{15}\right)^2 & =n_e \times 4 \times 10^{20} \\ \text{or,} \quad n_e & =(0.3 \times 1.2) \times 10^{10} \\ \therefore \quad n_e & =3.6 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~m}^{-3} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Two identical spring balances S1 and S2 are connected one after the other and are held vertically as shown in the figure. A mass of 10 kg is hanging from S2. If the readings on S1 and S2 are W1 and W2 respectively, then :
subject: Physics
solution:
Step 1:

Let the hanging mass be $m = 10 \,\text{kg}$.

The weight of the mass is

$$ W = mg = 10 \times 9.8 = 98 \,\text{N}.$$

For convenience, in such problems, instead of Newton, we express the balance reading directly in “kg‑weight.”

So, the force corresponds to 10 kg‑weight.

Step 2:

Spring balance S₂ is directly attached to the mass.

Therefore, S₂ supports the whole 10 kg mass.

So,

$$ W_2 = 10 \,\text{kg}. $$

Step 3:

Now look at spring balance S₁.

It is supporting both S₂ and the 10 kg mass hanging below it.

Thus, S₁ also bears the same total weight of $10 \,\text{kg}$.

So,

$$ W_1 = 10 \,\text{kg}. $$

Final Answer:

$$ W_1 = 10 \,\text{kg}, \quad W_2 = 10 \,\text{kg} $$

Correct Option: D ✅

questionText: A stone is thrown horizontally from the top of a 20 m high building with a speed of 12 m/s. It hits the ground at a distance R from the building. Taking g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup> and neglecting air resistance will give :
subject: Physics
solution:
1. Vertical motion

  • Initial vertical velocity $u_y = 0$

  • Height $h = 20\,$m

  • Acceleration $g = 10\,$m/s²

  • Use $$h = \tfrac12\,g\,t^2$$

    so $$20 = \tfrac12\cdot10\cdot t^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t^2 = 4 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 2\ \text{s}$$

  1. Horizontal motion

  • Horizontal speed $v_x = 12\,$m/s (constant)

  • Range $R = v_x \,t = 12 \times 2 = 24\,$m

Answer: R = 24 m (Option C)

questionText: A sphere of volume V is made of a material with lower density than water. While on Earth, it floats on water with its volume f<sub>1</sub>V (f<sub>1</sub> < 1) submerged. On the other hand, on a spaceship accelerating with acceleration a < g (g is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth) in outer space, its submerged volume in water is f<sub>2</sub>V. Then:
options: f2 = (1 - $\frac{a}{g}$) f1​
f2 > f1​
f2 = $\frac{a}{g}$ f1​
subject: Physics
solution:
1. Let the density of the sphere be ρ_s and that of water be ρ_w. Its mass is

    $$m = \rho_s V\,. $$

  2. On Earth (acceleration = g):

    Equilibrium ⇒ weight = buoyant force

    $$m g = (\text{mass of displaced water})\;g = \bigl(\rho_w f_1 V\bigr)\,g\,. $$

    ⇒ $$\rho_s V g = \rho_w f_1 V g \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f_1 = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}\,. $$

  3. In the spaceship accelerating at a (< g):

    Effective “gravity” = a for both sphere and water.

    Equilibrium ⇒ $m\,a =$ buoyant force = $(\rho_w f_2 V)\,a$.

    $$\rho_s V a = \rho_w f_2 V a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f_2 = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}\,. $$

  4. Hence

    $$f_2 = f_1\,. $$

Answer: Option A.

questionText: Two identical containers X and Y are connected at the bottom by a thin tube of negligible volume. The tube has a valve in it, as shown in the figure. Initially container X has a liquid filled up to height h in it and container Y is empty. When the valve is opened, both containers have equal amount of liquid in equilibrium. If the initial (before the valve is opened) potential energy of the liquid is P1 and the final potential energy is P2 then :
options: P1 = 4P2
P1 = 2P2
P1 = 8P2
subject: Physics
solution:
1. Let the cross-sectional area of each container be $A$ and the liquid density be $\rho$. Total mass of liquid initially in $X$ is

    $$m = \rho A h.$$

  2. Choose the bottom of the containers as the reference level ($y=0$).

    – Initial centre of mass of liquid in $X$ is at height $h/2$.

    – Initial potential energy

    $$P_1 = m g \,\frac{h}{2} = (\rho A h)\,g\,\frac{h}{2} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{2}.$$

  3. After opening the valve, the liquid distributes equally: each container has height $h/2$.

    – Mass in each container is $\rho A (h/2)$.

    – Centre of mass in each is at half that height, $\,\tfrac{h/2}{2}=h/4\,$.

    – Potential energy of liquid in one container:

    $$P_{\rm one} = \Bigl[\rho A \tfrac{h}{2}\Bigr] g \,\frac{h}{4} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{8}.$$

    – Total final potential energy in both containers:

    $$P_2 = 2\,P_{\rm one} = 2\cdot\frac{\rho g A h^2}{8} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{4}.$$

  4. Ratio

    $$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{\tfrac{\rho g A h^2}{2}}{\tfrac{\rho g A h^2}{4}} = \frac12\div\frac14 = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_1 = 2\,P_2.$$

Answer: Option C.

questionText: Two identical containers X and Y are connected at the bottom by a thin tube of negligible volume. The tube has a valve in it, as shown in the figure. questionText: A particle is moving in a circle of radius R with a constant speed v. Its average acceleration over the time when it moves over half the circle is :
subject: Physics
solution:
1. Average acceleration definition

    $\displaystyle \vec a_{\text{avg}}=\frac{\Delta \vec v}{\Delta t}$

  2. Change in velocity over half circle

    – Initial speed $v$, final speed $v$, but directions opposite

    – $\displaystyle |\Delta \vec v|=|\vec v_f-\vec v_i|=2v$

  3. Time to move half the circle

    – Path length $=\pi R$, speed $=v$

    – $\displaystyle \Delta t=\frac{\pi R}{v}$

  4. Magnitude of average acceleration

    $$a_{\text{avg}}=\frac{|\Delta \vec v|}{\Delta t} =\frac{2v}{\pi R/v} =\frac{2v^2}{\pi R}$$

Answer: $\displaystyle \frac{2v^2}{\pi R}$ (Option C)

questionText: Two forces of 5⋅0 N each are acting on a point mass. If the angle between the forces is 60°, then the net force acting on the point mass has magnitude close to :
subject: Physics
solution:
1. Given two forces each of magnitude $5.0\,$N, with angle between them $\theta=60^\circ$.

  2. By the law of cosines, the resultant $R$ satisfies

    $$R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2\,F_1\,F_2\cos\theta.$$

  3. Substitute $F_1=F_2=5.0\,$N and $\cos60^\circ=\tfrac12$:

    $$R^2 = 5^2 + 5^2 + 2\cdot5\cdot5\cdot\tfrac12 = 25 + 25 + 25 = 75.$$

  4. Therefore

    $$R = \sqrt{75} = 5\sqrt3 \approx 5\times1.732 = 8.66\ {\rm N}.$$

  5. Closest value: 8.6 N.

Answer: Option A (8.6 N).

questionText: A rectangle ABCD is kept in front of a concave mirror of focal length f with its corners A and B being, respectively, at distances 2f and 3f from the mirror with AB along the principal axis as shown in the figure. It forms an image A'B'C'D' in front of the mirror. What is the ratio of B'C' to A'D'?
subject: Physics
solution:
The Mirror Equation:

When rays emanating from a point meet at another point after reflection or refraction, that point is termed the image of the initial point.

The image is termed real if the rays actually converge at that point.

It is virtual if the rays do not actually meet but appear to diverge from that point when extended backward.

The mirror equation is given by: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u}$

Linear magnification (m) is defined as the ratio of the height of the image ($h'$) to the height of the object ($h$): $m = \frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u}$

Calculation:

Given rectangle ABCD with AD = BC ---- (1)

Corners A and B are at distances 2f and 3f from the mirror, respectively.

For corner A (u = 2f): $ \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{2f} \implies v = 2f $

Magnification: $ m = \frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{2f}{2f} = -1 $ This means the size of the image is equal to the size of the object: $ AD = A'D' ---- (2) $

For corner B (u = 3f): $ \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{3f} \implies v = \frac{3}{2}f $

Magnification: $ m = \frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{\frac{3}{2}f}{3f} = -\frac{1}{2} $ This means the size of the image is half the size of the object: $ B'C' = \frac{1}{2}BC ---- (3) $

From equations 1, 2, and 3, the ratio of B'C' to A'D' is: $ \frac{B'C'}{A'D'} = \frac{\frac{1}{2}BC}{AD} = \frac{1}{2} $

questionText: Who among the following is not a recipient of Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2022?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
1. Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2022 was awarded jointly to

    a. Carolyn R. Bertozzi

    b. Morten Meldal

    c. K. Barry Sharpless

  2. Benjamin List received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2021 (for organocatalysis), not in 2022.

  3. Therefore, the one who is not a 2022 recipient is Option B: Benjamin List.

questionText: Which of the hydrocarbons are arranged as per the increasing order of their boiling points?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To arrange hydrocarbons in order of increasing boiling points, we need to consider their molecular weights and structures. Generally, for alkanes (saturated hydrocarbons), the boiling point increases with the number of carbon atoms due to increased van der Waals forces.

Let's examine each option:

Option A: Methane, Butane, Propane, Heptane

Methane (CH₄) has the lowest boiling point.

Propane (C₃H₈) follows next.

Butane (C₄H₁₀) follows propane.

Heptane (C₇H₁₆) has the highest boiling point.

The order in this option is not correct.

Option B: Propane, Butane, Pentane, Octane

Propane (C₃H₈)

Butane (C₄H₁₀)

Pentane (C₅H₁₂)

Octane (C₈H₁₈)

This order is correct as it lists the hydrocarbons in order of increasing carbon number and boiling point.

Option C: Propane, Butane, Heptane, Methane

Methane should be first, not last, as it has the lowest boiling point.

This order is incorrect.

Option D: Octane, Ethane, Methane, Propane

Octane (C₈H₁₈) should have the highest boiling point, not the lowest.

This order is also incorrect.

Therefore, Option B is the correct choice, arranging the hydrocarbons in order of increasing boiling points: Propane, Butane, Pentane, Octane.

questionText: Which one of the following apparatus is used for separating benzene and water mixture?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The correct apparatus for separating a benzene and water mixture is:

Option C: Separating funnel

A separating funnel is specifically designed for separating immiscible liquids with different densities, such as benzene (an organic solvent) and water.

Here's a simple outline of how it works:

Fill the Separating Funnel: Pour the mixture into the separating funnel.

Allow to Settle: Let the mixture settle so that the two layers form.

The denser liquid (usually water) will settle at the bottom.

The less dense liquid (such as benzene) will be on top.

Drain the Layers: Open the stopcock to drain the bottom layer into a separate container.

Collect the Upper Layer: Finally, collect the upper layer in another container.

This process effectively separates the immiscible liquids based on their densities.

questionText: Who among the following is not a recipient of Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2022?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
1. Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2022 was awarded jointly to

    a. Carolyn R. Bertozzi

    b. Morten Meldal

    c. K. Barry Sharpless

  2. Benjamin List received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2021 (for organocatalysis), not in 2022.

  3. Therefore, the one who is not a 2022 recipient is Option B: Benjamin List.

questionText: Which of the hydrocarbons are arranged as per the increasing order of their boiling points?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To arrange hydrocarbons in order of increasing boiling points, we need to consider their molecular weights and structures. Generally, for alkanes (saturated hydrocarbons), the boiling point increases with the number of carbon atoms due to increased van der Waals forces.

Let's examine each option:

Option A: Methane, Butane, Propane, Heptane

Methane (CH₄) has the lowest boiling point.

Propane (C₃H₈) follows next.

Butane (C₄H₁₀) follows propane.

Heptane (C₇H₁₆) has the highest boiling point.

The order in this option is not correct.

Option B: Propane, Butane, Pentane, Octane

Propane (C₃H₈)

Butane (C₄H₁₀)

Pentane (C₅H₁₂)

Octane (C₈H₁₈)

This order is correct as it lists the hydrocarbons in order of increasing carbon number and boiling point.

Option C: Propane, Butane, Heptane, Methane

Methane should be first, not last, as it has the lowest boiling point.

This order is incorrect.

Option D: Octane, Ethane, Methane, Propane

Octane (C₈H₁₈) should have the highest boiling point, not the lowest.

This order is also incorrect.

Therefore, Option B is the correct choice, arranging the hydrocarbons in order of increasing boiling points: Propane, Butane, Pentane, Octane.

questionText: Which one of the following apparatus is used for separating benzene and water mixture?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The correct apparatus for separating a benzene and water mixture is:

Option C: Separating funnel

A separating funnel is specifically designed for separating immiscible liquids with different densities, such as benzene (an organic solvent) and water.

Here's a simple outline of how it works:

Fill the Separating Funnel: Pour the mixture into the separating funnel.

Allow to Settle: Let the mixture settle so that the two layers form.

The denser liquid (usually water) will settle at the bottom.

The less dense liquid (such as benzene) will be on top.

Drain the Layers: Open the stopcock to drain the bottom layer into a separate container.

Collect the Upper Layer: Finally, collect the upper layer in another container.

This process effectively separates the immiscible liquids based on their densities.

questionText: An iron nail dipped in copper sulphate solution turns brown. This is due to which one of the following types of reactions?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The iron nail turning brown when dipped in copper sulphate solution is due to the following reaction:

$$ \text{Fe (s)} + \text{CuSO}_4 \text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 \text{(aq)} + \text{Cu (s)} $$

In this reaction, iron displaces copper from copper sulphate solution, forming iron sulphate and depositing copper metal on the nail, which is observed as a brown layer.

Thus, the correct type of reaction is:

Option D: Displacement reaction

subject: Chemistry
solution:

Thus, the correct type of reaction is:

Option D: Displacement reaction

questionText: Toothpaste prevents tooth decay by:
options: making a coat of calcium over the teeth surface.
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Toothpaste helps prevent tooth decay primarily through:

Option C: the action of fluoride.

Fluoride in toothpaste helps to strengthen tooth enamel and make it more resistant to the acids produced by bacteria in the mouth, which can cause decay. Additionally, some toothpastes may help to neutralize acids (Option A), but the primary mechanism is through the remineralization effect of fluoride.

questionText: Which one of the following statements about X-rays is not true?
options: They have wavelengths of about 1 Å.
These can be generated by bombarding a metal target by high energy electrons.
Due to their wavelengths being shorter, these can be used for radar systems.
These are also used for the treatment of certain forms of cancer.
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The statement that is not true about X-rays is:

Option C: Due to their wavelengths being shorter, these can be used for radar systems.

X-rays, due to their very small wavelengths (on the order of 1 Å), are not suitable for radar systems. Radar systems typically use radio waves or microwaves, which have much longer wavelengths. The shorter wavelengths of X-rays make them suitable for imaging and medical applications, but not for radar.

questionText: Mica is mainly used in:
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The correct answer is:

Option D: electrical and electronic industries.

Mica is predominantly used in the electrical and electronic industries due to its excellent insulating properties and high dielectric strength. It is commonly found in components such as capacitors, insulators, and various other electrical equipment.

questionText: The streaming of light beams coming from the Sun through trees is said to have suggested that light travels in straight line. The particles on the path of light beams are visible to us because
options: dust particles in the air reflect light into our eyes
dust particles in the air scatter light into our eyes
dust particles in the air refract light into our eyes
dust particles in the air polarize light into our eyes
subject: Physics
solution:
The phenomenon you are describing is commonly observed, especially when sunlight streams through forest canopies or through windows into a dusty room. This effect provides evidence that light travels in straight lines and also makes the particles in the light path visible to the observer.

Among the provided options:

Option A: suggests that dust particles in the air reflect light into our eyes. While reflection does involve bouncing light off surfaces, in this context, it is not the primary reason why we see the particles.

Option B: suggests that dust particles in the air scatter light into our eyes. This is the correct explanation. Scattering occurs when light is deflected in many different directions by small particles. When sunlight strikes dust particles, the light is scattered in various directions, making the dust particles visible from multiple angles. This scattered light enters our eyes, allowing us to see the particles.

Option C: suggests that dust particles in the air refract light into our eyes. Refraction refers to the bending of light as it passes through different media, which is not the primary mechanism here.

Option D: suggests that dust particles in the air polarize light into our eyes. Polarization refers to the orientation of light waves in certain directions, which is also not the primary reason for visibility in this scenario.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B: dust particles in the air scatter light into our eyes.

questionText: The flash of lightning is seen before the thunderstorm is heard. It verifies that
options: sound travels much faster than light
light travels much faster than sound
light and sound both travel with same speed
intensity of flash of lightning is very high during thunderstorm
subject: Physics
solution:
The phenomenon where the flash of lightning is seen before the thunderstorm is heard can be explained by the difference in the speeds at which light and sound travel. Light travels significantly faster than sound. Thus, the correct option is:

Option B: light travels much faster than sound

To elaborate, light travels at an approximate speed of $$3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$$ in a vacuum, and slightly slower in the atmosphere. On the other hand, sound travels at a much slower speed of approximately $$343 \, \text{m/s}$$ in air at room temperature. Because of this large discrepancy in speeds, the light from the lightning is observed almost instantaneously, while the sound waves take a longer time to reach the observer's ears. This delay is why we see the flash of lightning before we hear the thunder.

questionText: The potential difference between the two end terminals of an electric heater is 220 V and the current through it is 0.5 A. What would be the current through the heater if the potential difference across the terminals of the heater is reduced to 120 V?
subject: Physics
solution:
To determine the current through the heater when the potential difference is reduced to 120 V, we can use Ohm's Law, which states:

$$ V = IR $$

where:

  • $$ V $$ is the potential difference (voltage)
  • $$ I $$ is the current
  • $$ R $$ is the resistance

First, we need to find the resistance of the heater using the initial conditions. Given that the initial potential difference $$ V_1 $$ is 220 V, and the initial current $$ I_1 $$ is 0.5 A:

$$ R = \frac{V_1}{I_1} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ R = \frac{220 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ A}} $$ = 440 $$ \Omega $$

Now, we need to find the current $$ I_2 $$ when the potential difference across the heater, $$ V_2 $$, is reduced to 120 V using the same resistance. By applying Ohm's Law again:

$$ I_2 = \frac{V_2}{R} $$

Substituting the new potential difference and the resistance:

$$ I_2 = \frac{120 \text{ V}}{440 \Omega} $$

Calculating the value:

$$ I_2 \approx 0.273 \text{ A} $$

Therefore, the current through the heater would be approximately 0.27 A when the potential difference is 120 V.

The correct answer is:

Option C

0.27 A

questionText: The heating element in an electric iron is usually made of
subject: Physics
solution:
The heating element in an electric iron is typically made of a material that has a high resistance to generate sufficient heat when electric current passes through it. Among the options provided, the best choice for this application is Nichrome, which is a nickel-chromium alloy. Nichrome is preferred because it has a high melting point, excellent resistance to oxidation, and stable resistance over a wide range of temperatures.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option C Nichrome

To elaborate more, when electric current flows through Nichrome, it encounters resistance, converting electrical energy into heat energy. This principle of Joule heating is utilized in various heating applications, such as in electric irons, toasters, and hair dryers. The equation governing this process is given by:

$$Q = I^2 R t$$

where:

  • $$Q$$ is the heat energy produced in joules
  • $$I$$ is the current in amperes
  • $$R$$ is the resistance in ohms
  • $$t$$ is the time in seconds

Using Nichrome allows manufacturers to create reliable and efficient heating elements that can withstand high temperatures and prolonged usage.

questionText: Sita, 1.5 m high, stands before a plane mirror fixed on a wall to view her full image. What should be the minimum height of the plane mirror so that Sita can view her image fully?
subject: Physics
solution:
To determine the minimum height of the plane mirror required for Sita to view her full image, we need to understand the properties of a plane mirror. A plane mirror reflects light in such a way that the image formed is virtual, upright, and of the same size as the object. In order for her to see her complete image, the length of the mirror must be half the height of Sita.

Here's why: When Sita looks at her reflection in the mirror, the topmost and bottommost rays from her body will reflect off the mirror at equal angles. To capture both her head and feet in the reflection, the mirror needs to be at least half her height. This is because each point on Sita's body is reflected in the corresponding point on the mirror at half the height, allowing the complete image to fit.

So, if Sita's height is 1.5 m, the minimum height of the mirror should be:

$$\frac{1.5 \, \text{m}}{2} = 0.75 \, \text{m}$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option C: 0.75 m

questionText: Which one of the following graphs correctly represents the current (I) - voltage (V) variation for a rectangular piece of a semiconductor wafer?
subject: Physics
solution: The current flowing through the semiconductor is not linearly proportional to the voltage. In forward bias the current increases by increasing the voltage in parabolic way.

questionText: Which one of the following is the usual unit of measurement for Air Pressure used in India?
subject: Physics
solution:
The correct answer is Option B: Millibar. Here's why:

Air pressure is the weight of the atmosphere pressing down on a given area. It's measured as the force per unit area. Here's a breakdown of why the other options are incorrect and why millibars are used:

  • Centimeter (Option A): Centimeters measure length or distance, not pressure.
  • Milligram (Option C): Milligrams measure mass, not pressure.
  • Milliliter (Option D): Milliliters measure volume, not pressure.
  • Millibar (Option B): Millibars are the standard unit of measurement for air pressure in India and many other parts of the world. A millibar is a unit of pressure equal to 1/1000 of a bar.

In India, weather reports and forecasts typically use millibars (mb) to indicate air pressure. Other units of measurement for air pressure exist (like inches of mercury), but millibars are the most common in India.

questionText: Galvanization is a method of protecting iron from rusting by coating with a thin layer of
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The correct answer is Option C: Zinc. Here's why:

Galvanization is a process where a thin layer of zinc is applied to iron or steel to prevent rust. This protection works due to the following principles:

  • Sacrificial Protection: Zinc is more reactive than iron. When exposed to the environment, zinc will oxidize (react with oxygen) before the iron. This means the zinc layer acts as a sacrificial anode, corroding instead of the iron, thus protecting the underlying metal.
  • Barrier Protection: The zinc coating forms a physical barrier that prevents oxygen and moisture from reaching the iron surface, which are essential for rust formation.

Let's look at why the other options are incorrect:

  • Option A: Gallium: Gallium is a metal, but it's not commonly used for galvanization. It's primarily used in electronics and other specialized applications.
  • Option B: Aluminum: Aluminum can also provide some protection against corrosion, but it's not as effective as zinc for galvanization. Aluminum forms a very thin oxide layer that can be easily damaged, making it less protective than a zinc coating.
  • Option D: Silver: Silver is a noble metal, meaning it's resistant to corrosion. However, it's too expensive and not as effective as zinc for protecting iron in everyday applications.

In summary, zinc is the most effective and widely used metal for galvanization due to its sacrificial and barrier protection properties. This makes it the best choice for protecting iron from rust.

questionText: Which one of the following alloys contains a non-metal as one of its constituent?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To answer this question, let's examine the composition of each alloy mentioned:

Option A: Brass

Brass is an alloy primarily composed of copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn). Both copper and zinc are metals. Therefore, brass does not contain a non-metal as one of its constituents.

Option B: Bronze

Bronze is an alloy primarily composed of copper (Cu) and tin (Sn). Both copper and tin are metals. Therefore, bronze does not contain a non-metal as one of its constituents.

Option C: Amalgam

Amalgam is an alloy that contains mercury (Hg) and one or more other metals. Amalgams are commonly used in dental fillings. Since all the constituents are metals, amalgam does not contain a non-metal as one of its constituents.

Option D: Steel

Steel is an alloy primarily composed of iron (Fe) and carbon (C). Here, iron is a metal while carbon is a non-metal. Therefore, steel contains a non-metal (carbon) as one of its constituents.

So, the correct answer is:

Option D: Steel

questionText: Buckminster fullerene, which looks like a football, is an allotropic form of
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Buckminster fullerene, also known as C60, is a spherical molecule made entirely of carbon atoms arranged in a pattern that resembles a soccer ball (football). The structure consists of 60 carbon atoms forming 12 pentagons and 20 hexagons, creating a highly stable and symmetrical shape. This particular molecular form of carbon falls under the group of compounds known as fullerenes, which are allotropes of carbon.

In chemistry, allotropes are different structural forms of the same element, where atoms are bonded together in different ways. For carbon, other notable allotropes include diamond, graphite, and graphene. Buckminster fullerene is an exciting form due to its unique properties and potential applications in materials science and nanotechnology.

Given the options:

Option A: Phosphorus

Option B: Sulfur

Option C: Carbon

Option D: Tin

The correct answer is Option C: Carbon.

Thus, Buckminster fullerene is an allotropic form of carbon.

questionText:
Consider the following reaction:

Fe2O3(s) + 2Al(s) → 2Fe(s) + Al2O3(s)

Which of the following statements about the given reaction is NOT correct?
options: It is an example of displacement reaction
Fe2O3 acts as oxidizing agent
This reaction is applicable to joining of railway tracks
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Let's analyze the given reaction:

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3(\text{s}) + 2\text{Al}(\text{s}) \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}(\text{s}) + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3(\text{s})$$

Now, let's evaluate each of the given statements:

Option A: It is an example of a displacement reaction.

This is a true statement. In this reaction, aluminum (Al) displaces iron (Fe) from iron(III) oxide (Fe2O3), forming aluminum oxide (Al2O3) and iron (Fe). This is a characteristic of a displacement reaction.

Option B: It is a highly endothermic reaction.

This statement is incorrect. The reaction between iron(III) oxide and aluminum is actually highly exothermic. It releases a large amount of heat, enough to melt iron. This is why it is used in thermite welding, which is utilized to join railway tracks.

Option C: Fe2O3 acts as an oxidizing agent.

This is a true statement. In this reaction, Fe2O3 is reduced to Fe, and in the process, it oxidizes aluminum (Al) to Al2O3. Thus, Fe2O3 acts as an oxidizing agent.

Option D: This reaction is applicable to the joining of railway tracks.

This statement is true. The highly exothermic nature of the reaction makes it suitable for welding railway tracks together in a process known as thermite welding.

Therefore, the statement that is NOT correct is:

Option B: It is a highly endothermic reaction.

questionText:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists:

LIST I

(Chemical process)

LIST II

(Reaction)

A.

Electrolysis of water

1.

Double displacement
reaction

B.

Burning of coal

2.

Combination reaction

C.

Iron nail immersed in copper sulphate solution

3.

Decomposition reaction

D.

Addition of barium chloride solution to aluminium sulphate solution

4.

Displacement reaction
subject: Chemistry
solution:
Let's match the given chemical processes in List I with their corresponding reactions in List II.

List I:

  • A. Electrolysis of water
  • B. Burning of coal
  • C. Iron nail immersed in copper sulphate solution
  • D. Addition of barium chloride solution to aluminium sulphate solution

List II:

  • 1. Double displacement reaction
  • 2. Combination reaction
  • 3. Decomposition reaction
  • 4. Displacement reaction

Now, let's determine the correct matches:

A. Electrolysis of water: This process involves breaking down water ($$H_2O$$) into hydrogen ($$H_2$$) and oxygen ($$O_2$$) gases. This is an example of a decomposition reaction.

$$2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$$

Match: A - 3

B. Burning of coal: Burning coal, which is primarily carbon ($$C$$), in the presence of oxygen ($$O_2$$) results in carbon dioxide ($$CO_2$$). This is a combination reaction.

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

Match: B - 2

C. Iron nail immersed in copper sulphate solution: The iron ($$Fe$$) displaces copper ($$Cu$$) from copper sulphate ($$CuSO_4$$) solution, forming iron sulphate ($$FeSO_4$$) and copper. This is a displacement reaction.

$$Fe + CuSO_4 \rightarrow FeSO_4 + Cu$$

Match: C - 4

D. Addition of barium chloride solution to aluminium sulphate solution: Mixing barium chloride ($$BaCl_2$$) with aluminium sulphate ($$Al_2(SO_4)_3$$) leads to the formation of barium sulphate ($$BaSO_4$$) and aluminium chloride ($$AlCl_3$$). This is a double displacement reaction.

$$3BaCl_2 + Al_2(SO_4)_3 \rightarrow 3BaSO_4 + 2AlCl_3$$

Match: D - 1

Therefore, the correct matching is:

  • A - 3
  • B - 2
  • C - 4
  • D - 1

So, the correct option is:

Option C: A - 3, B - 2, C - 4, D - 1

questionText: Which one of the following processes involves chemical reaction?
options: Storing of oxygen gas under pressure in a gas cylinder
Burning of magnesium ribbon in air
subject: Chemistry
solution:
The process which involves a chemical reaction is the burning of magnesium ribbon in air.

Let's examine each option to understand why:

Option A: Evaporation of petrol

Evaporation is a physical change where a liquid turns into a gas. There are no changes in the chemical composition of petrol during this process. The molecules in petrol simply gain enough energy to change their state from liquid to gas without altering their structure.

Option B: Dissolution of salt in water

When salt (sodium chloride, NaCl) dissolves in water, it dissociates into sodium ions (Na+) and chloride ions (Cl-). This is a physical process because there is no change in the chemical properties of the individual ions, and no new substances are formed.

Option C: Storing of oxygen gas under pressure in a gas cylinder

Storing oxygen gas under pressure in a gas cylinder is a physical process. The oxygen molecules (O2) remain the same, only their state of arrangement and pressure changes. There is no chemical reaction or formation of new substances involved.

Option D: Burning of magnesium ribbon in air

When magnesium ribbon burns in air, it reacts with oxygen to form magnesium oxide (MgO). This is a chemical reaction because the original substances (magnesium and oxygen) combine at the atomic level to form a new substance with different chemical properties. The chemical reaction can be represented by the following equation:

$$\text{2 Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2 MgO}$$

Hence, Option D: Burning of magnesium ribbon in air involves a chemical reaction.

questionText: Which one of the following metals does NOT react with cold water to liberate hydrogen gas?
subject: Chemistry
solution:
To answer the question, let's examine the reactivity of each metal with cold water.

Potassium (Option A): Potassium is a very reactive metal. When it comes into contact with cold water, it reacts vigorously to form potassium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. The reaction is represented by:

$$ 2K + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2KOH + H_2 \uparrow $$

Sodium (Option D): Sodium is also highly reactive with cold water. The reaction produces sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. The chemical equation for this reaction is:

$$ 2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow $$

Calcium (Option C): Calcium reacts with cold water, though less vigorously than potassium and sodium. The reaction produces calcium hydroxide and hydrogen gas:

$$ Ca + 2H_2O \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2 + H_2 \uparrow $$

Iron (Option B): Iron does not react with cold water to any significant extent to liberate hydrogen gas. The reaction between iron and water typically requires higher temperatures or the presence of steam.

Therefore, the metal that does NOT react with cold water to liberate hydrogen gas is:

Option B: Iron

questionText: The derivative of $$x^{2 x}$$ w.r.t. $$x$$ is:
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the derivative of $$x^{2 x}$$ with respect to $$x$$, we use the logarithmic differentiation technique due to the variable exponents. Let's define the function as $$y = x^{2x}$$ and then differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$.

First, we take the natural logarithm of both sides to simplify differentiation:

$$\ln{y} = \ln{(x^{2x})}$$

Using properties of logarithms, we can simplify the right hand side:

$$\ln{y} = 2x\ln{x}$$

Next, differentiate both sides with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2x\cdot\frac{1}{x}$$

Simplifying gives us:

$$\frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = 2\ln{x} + 2$$

Remember that $$y = x^{2x}$$, so substituting back gives us:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = x^{2x}\left(2\ln{x} + 2\right)$$

Therefore, the derivative of $$x^{2x}$$ with respect to $$x$$ is $$2x^{2x}(1 + \log{x})$$.

Thus, the correct answer is Option C: $$2x^{2x}(1+\log{x})$$.

questionText: The interval in which the function $$f(x)=2 x^3+9 x^2 +12 x-1$$ is decreasing is :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =2 x^3+9 x^2+12 x-1 \\\\ f^{\prime}(x) & =6 x^2+18 x+12-0 \end{aligned}$$

for decreasing function $$f^{\prime}(x)<0$$

$$\begin{aligned} 6\left(x^2+3 x+2\right) & <0 \\\\ 6(x+2)(x+1) & <0 \end{aligned}$$

$$f(x) \text { is decreasing in interval }(-2,-1)$$

questionText: The function $$f(x)=[x]$$, where $$[x]$$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $$x$$, is continuous at:
subject: Mathematics
solution: The function $$f(x)=[x]$$ is continuous for all except all integral values of $$x$$.

questionText:
(a) The median of an equilateral triangle is increasing at the rate of $$2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$. Find the rate at which its side is increasing.

OR

(b) Sum of two numbers is 5. If the sum of the cubes of these numbers is least, then find the sum of the squares of these numbers.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let the length of the median be x cm.

and side of equilateral triangle be $$y \mathrm{~cm}$$

In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABD}$$

$$\begin{aligned} A B^2 & =A D^2+B D^2\left(\angle D=90^{\circ}\right) \\\\ y^2 & =x^2+\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3}{4} y^2 & =x^2 \\\\ y^2 & =\frac{4}{3} x^2 \\\\ y & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} x \\\\ \frac{d}{d t}(y) & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{d}{d t}(x) \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{d x}{d t} \\\\ \frac{d y}{d t} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times 2 \sqrt{3}\\\\ &=4 ~\text{cm/s} \end{aligned}$$

Hence side of equilateral triangle be increase at $$4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$$

OR

(b) Let the number be $$x$$ and $$y$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad x+y & =5 \quad \text{.... (1)}\\\\ S & =x^3+y^3 \quad \text{.... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

from (1) & (2)

$$\begin{aligned} S & =x^3+(5-x)^3 \\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-3(5-x)^2 \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =3 x^2-75+30 x-3 x^2 \\\\ & =-75+30 x \end{aligned}$$

for maximum and minimum

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d S}{d x} & =0 \\ -75+30 x & =0 \\ x & =\frac{75}{30}=\frac{5}{2} \\\\ \frac{d S}{d x} & =-75+30 x \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & =30 \\\\ \frac{d^2 S}{d x^2} & >0 \end{aligned}$$

Hence $$S$$ is minimum at $$x=\frac{5}{2}$$

Minimum value of $$x^2+y^2$$

$$=\frac{25}{4}+\frac{25}{4}=\frac{25}{2}$$

questionText: Sketch the region bounded by the lines $$2 x+y=8, y=2, y=4$$ and the $$y$$-axis. Hence, obtain its area using integration.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$\begin{aligned} 2 x+y & =8 \\ y & =2 \\ y & =4 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Required Area } & =\int_2^4 x d y=\int_2^4 \frac{8-y}{2} d y \\\\ & =\left[4 y-\frac{y^2}{4}\right]_2^4 \\\\ & =[16-4-8+1] \\\\ \text { Required area } & =5 \text { unit }^2 \end{aligned}$$

questionText:
Find the area of the following region using integration:

$$\left\{(x, y): y^2 \leq 2 x \text { and } y \geq x-4\right\}$$
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given:

$$\begin{aligned} y^2 & =2 x \quad \text{.... (i)}\\\\ y & =x-4 \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Required area is OABCO

From (1) and (2)

$$\begin{aligned} &(x-4)^2=2 x \\\\ & x^2-10 x+16=0 \\\\ &(x-8)(x-2)=0 \\\\ & x=8 \text { and } x=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text { Intersection points }(2,-2) \text { and }(8,4)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Required Area } & =\left[\frac{y^2}{2}+4 y-\frac{y^3}{6}\right]_{-2}^4 \\\\ & =\left(8+16-\frac{32}{3}-2+8-\frac{4}{3}\right) \\\\ & =30-12 \\\\ & =18 \text { unit }^2 \end{aligned}$$

questionText:
(a) If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}x^2, & \text { if } x \geq 1 \\ x, & \text { if } x<1\end{array}\right.$$, then show that $$f$$ is not differentiable at $$x=1$$.

OR

(b) Find the value(s) of '$$\lambda$$', if the function

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array} \text { is continuous at } x=0 .\right.$$
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^2, & \text { if } x \geq 1 \\ x, & \text { if } x<1 \end{array}\right.$$

$$f(x)$$ is defined at $$x=1$$ and $$f(1)=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} f_{-}^{\prime}(1) & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\ & =\frac{1+h-1}{h}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} f_{+}^{\prime}(1) & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+h)^2-1}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2+h)}{h} \\ f_{-}^{\prime}(1) & \neq f_{+}^{\prime}(1) \end{aligned}$$

Hence $$f(x)$$ is not differentiable at $$x=1$$

OR

$$\text { (b) } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array} \text { is continuous at } x=0 .\right.$$

$$\begin{aligned} f(0) & =1 \\ \substack{f(x) \\ x \rightarrow 0^{-}} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda(0-h)}{(0-h)^2} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda h}{h^2} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \lambda^2\left(\frac{\sin \lambda h}{\lambda h}\right)^2 \\ & =\lambda^2 \times 1=\lambda^2 \\ f(0) & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \\ 1 & =\lambda^2 \therefore \lambda= \pm 1 \end{aligned}$$

questionText: If $$x=A \cos 4 t+B \sin 4 t$$, then $$\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}$$ is equal to:
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given the function $$x = A \cos 4t + B \sin 4t$$, we want to find the second derivative of $$x$$ with respect to $$t$$, signified as $$\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}$$. To do so, let's follow the standard procedure of differentiation.

First, we find the first derivative of $$x$$ with respect to $$t$$, denoted as $$\frac{dx}{dt}$$.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = -4A\sin 4t + 4B\cos 4t$$

This uses the basic differentiation rules that $$\frac{d}{dt}(\cos kt) = -k\sin kt$$ and $$\frac{d}{dt}(\sin kt) = k\cos kt$$, where $$k$$ is a constant.

Next, we differentiate $$\frac{dx}{dt}$$ once more to find the second derivative of $$x$$ with respect to $$t$$, denoted as $$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$.

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -16A\cos 4t - 16B\sin 4t$$

Here we apply differentiation rules again, obtaining $$-4(-4A\sin 4t) = 16A\cos 4t$$ and $$4(4B\cos 4t) = 16B\sin 4t$$. Consequently, the terms obtain a factor of $$-16$$.

Now, compare $$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$ with the original function $$x = A\cos 4t + B\sin 4t$$. Notice that $$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$ can be re-written to highlight its relation with $$x$$ as follows:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -16(A\cos 4t + B\sin 4t)$$

We recognize that $$A\cos 4t + B\sin 4t$$ is our original function $$x$$. Thus,

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -16x$$

This shows that the second derivative of $$x$$ with respect to $$t$$ is $$-16x$$. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$-16 x$$

questionText: $$\int_\limits{-1}^1 \frac{|x-2|}{x-2} d x, x \neq 2 \text { is equal to: }$$
subject: Mathematics
solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \int_\limits{-1}^1 \frac{|x-2|}{x-2} d x \\ & \quad \int_\limits{-1}^1 \frac{-(x-2)}{x-2} d x=[-x]_{-1}^1=-2 \end{aligned}$$

questionText:
Assertion (A): $$\int_\limits2^8 \frac{\sqrt{10-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}} d x=3$$

Reason (R): $$\int_\limits a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x$$
options: (a) Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
(b) Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of A.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let's analyze Assertion (A) and Reason (R) individually and then see the relationship between them to determine which option is correct.

Evaluating Assertion (A):

To evaluate the assertion, we will directly compute the integral given in Assertion (A).

$$\int_\limits2^8 \frac{\sqrt{10-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}} dx$$

Let's use the property mentioned in Reason (R), $$\int_a^b f(x) dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) dx$$, to simplify the integral. Here, $a=2$, $b=8$, and $f(x) = \frac{\sqrt{10-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}}$.

Applying the property:

$$\int_\limits2^8 \frac{\sqrt{10-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}} dx = \int_\limits2^8 \frac{\sqrt{8+2-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{8+2-x}} dx = \int_\limits2^8 \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}} dx$$

If we add the original integral and the transformed integral, we get:

$$2I = \int_\limits2^8 [\frac{\sqrt{10-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}} + \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{10-x}}] dx$$

Simplifying the integrand:

$$2I = \int_\limits2^8 1 dx$$

$$2I = [x]_2^8 = 8-2 = 6$$

Therefore, $I = \frac{6}{2} = 3$. Thus, Assertion (A) is true.

Evaluating Reason (R):

The property $ \int_a^b f(x) dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) dx $ is a well-known property in integral calculus, which states that the integral of a function over a symmetric interval can be computed by substituting $x$ with $a + b - x$. This property is true, so Reason (R) is indeed true.

Relation Between (A) and (R):

Since we used Reason (R) to directly evaluate Assertion (A) and show that it is true, Reason (R) is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).

Therefore, the correct option is (a) Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).

questionText: $$\text { If }\left|\begin{array}{lll} \alpha & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right|=0 \text {, then the value of } \alpha \text { is: }$$
subject: Mathematics
solution: $$\begin{gathered} {\left[\begin{array}{lll} \alpha & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right]=0} \\ \Rightarrow \alpha(2-4)-3(1-1)+4(4-2)=0 \\ -2 \alpha+8=0 \\ \alpha=4 \end{gathered}$$

questionText:
Consider the following statements :

1. f(x) = In x is increasing in (0, ∞)

2. $g(x)=e^x+e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing in (0, ∞)

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine which of the statements are correct, let's analyze each one in detail:

Statement 1: $ f(x) = \ln x $ is increasing in $ (0, \infty) $

To verify if $ f(x) = \ln x $ is increasing, we need to find the derivative of $ f(x) $ and check its sign in the interval $ (0, \infty) $.

The derivative of $ f(x) = \ln x $ is given by:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} (\ln x) = \frac{1}{x} $$

In the interval $ (0, \infty) $, $ x $ is always positive, therefore $ \frac{1}{x} $ is always positive. Hence, $ f(x) = \ln x $ is an increasing function in $ (0, \infty) $.

Thus, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing in $ (0, \infty) $

To verify if $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is decreasing, we need to find the derivative of $ g(x) $ and check its sign in the interval $ (0, \infty) $.

The derivative of $ g(x) $ is given by:

$$ g'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \left( e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} \right) $$

Applying the chain rule to the second term, we get:

$$ g'(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{x^2} \right) = e^x - \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} $$

To determine if $ g'(x) $ is negative in $ (0, \infty) $, we need to check if:

$$ e^x - \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} < 0 $$

However, for $ x > 0 $, $ e^x $ grows exponentially, and $ e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is always positive. Thus, $ e^x $ will always be larger than $ \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}{x^2} $ for $ x > 1 $. This indicates that $ g'(x) $ is generally positive in $ (0, \infty) $.

Therefore, $ g(x) = e^x + e^{\frac{1}{x}} $ is not a decreasing function in $ (0, \infty) $.

Thus, Statement 2 is incorrect.

Conclusion:

The correct answer is:

Option A: 1 only

questionText: What is the derivative of sin<sup>2</sup> x with respect to cos<sup>2</sup>x ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$\cos^2 x$$, we need to use the concept of implicit differentiation. We will first express the derivatives of these functions with respect to $$x$$ and then use the chain rule.

First, let's find the derivatives of $$\sin^2 x$$ and $$\cos^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$:

The derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$ is found using the chain rule:

$$\frac{d}{dx} (\sin^2 x) = 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x = \sin 2x$$

The derivative of $$\cos^2 x$$ with respect to $$x$$ is also found using the chain rule:

$$\frac{d}{dx} (\cos^2 x) = 2 \cos x \cdot (-\sin x) = -\sin 2x$$

Now we need to find the derivative of $$\sin^2 x$$ with respect to $$\cos^2 x$$. By using the chain rule in the form:

$$\frac{d}{d(\cos^2 x)} (\sin^2 x) = \frac{\frac{d}{dx} (\sin^2 x)}{\frac{d}{dx} (\cos^2 x)}$$

Substituting the derivatives we calculated earlier, we get:

$$\frac{d}{d(\cos^2 x)} (\sin^2 x) = \frac{\sin 2x}{-\sin 2x} = -1$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: -1.

questionText: For what value of m with m < 0, is the area bounded by the lines y = x, y = mx and x = 2 equal to 3 ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine the value of $$m$$ for which the area bounded by the lines $$y = x$$, $$y = mx$$, and $$x = 2$$ is equal to 3, we need to find the area of the triangle formed by these lines. The vertices of the triangle are the points of intersection of these lines.

The lines intersect at the following points:

  • $$y = x$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2)$$.
  • $$y = mx$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2m)$$.
  • The lines $$y = x$$ and $$y = mx$$ intersect at the origin $$(0, 0)$$ because at this point, $$y = x = mx$$ implies $$x(1 - m) = 0$$, so $$x = 0$$.

The area of the triangle can be found using the formula for the area of a triangle with vertices at $$(x_1, y_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

Substituting the coordinates of the vertices $$(0, 0)$$, $$(2, 2)$$, and $$(2, 2m)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0(2 - 2m) + 2(2m - 0) + 2(0 - 2) \right| $$

Simplifying inside the absolute value:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 2(2m) + 2(-2) \right| $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| $$

Since we are given that the area is 3, we set up the equation:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 3 $$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to eliminate the fraction:

$$ \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 6 $$

This absolute value equation splits into two cases:

1. $$4m - 4 = 6$$

2. $$4m - 4 = -6$$

Solving the first case:

$$ 4m - 4 = 6 $$

$$ 4m = 10 $$

$$ m = \frac{5}{2} $$

Since this is positive, it does not satisfy $$m < 0$$.

Solving the second case:

$$ 4m - 4 = -6 $$

$$ 4m = -2 $$

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

This value is negative and satisfies $$m < 0$$.

Therefore, the correct value of $$m$$ is

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

The correct option is $$- \frac{1}{2}$$.

questionText: For what value of m with m < 0, is the area bounded by the lines y = x, y = mx and x = 2 equal to 3 ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine the value of $$m$$ for which the area bounded by the lines $$y = x$$, $$y = mx$$, and $$x = 2$$ is equal to 3, we need to find the area of the triangle formed by these lines. The vertices of the triangle are the points of intersection of these lines.

The lines intersect at the following points:

  • $$y = x$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2)$$.
  • $$y = mx$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2m)$$.
  • The lines $$y = x$$ and $$y = mx$$ intersect at the origin $$(0, 0)$$ because at this point, $$y = x = mx$$ implies $$x(1 - m) = 0$$, so $$x = 0$$.

The area of the triangle can be found using the formula for the area of a triangle with vertices at $$(x_1, y_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

Substituting the coordinates of the vertices $$(0, 0)$$, $$(2, 2)$$, and $$(2, 2m)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0(2 - 2m) + 2(2m - 0) + 2(0 - 2) \right| $$

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 2(2m) + 2(-2) \right| $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| $$

Since we are given that the area is 3, we set up the equation:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 3 $$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to eliminate the fraction:

$$ \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 6 $$

This absolute value equation splits into two cases:

1. $$4m - 4 = 6$$

2. $$4m - 4 = -6$$

Solving the first case:

$$ 4m - 4 = 6 $$

$$ 4m = 10 $$

$$ m = \frac{5}{2} $$

Since this is positive, it does not satisfy $$m < 0$$.

Solving the second case:

$$ 4m - 4 = -6 $$

$$ 4m = -2 $$

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

This value is negative and satisfies $$m < 0$$.

Therefore, the correct value of $$m$$ is

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

The correct option is $$- \frac{1}{2}$$.

questionText: If the sixth term in the binomial expansion of $\left(x^{-\frac{8}{3}}+x^2 \log _{10} x\right)^8$ is 5600, then what is the value of x ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The binomial expansion of $$(a + b)^n$$ is given by:

$$ \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} a^{n-k} b^k $$

For the given expression $$\left(x^{-\frac{8}{3}} + x^2 \log_{10} x \right)^8$$, the sixth term corresponds to $$k = 5$$ because the general term $$T_{k+1}$$ corresponds to the term with $$k$$ in the binomial expansion. Here, $$a = x^{-\frac{8}{3}}$$, $$b = x^2 \log_{10} x$$ and $$n=8$$. The sixth term ($$T_6$$) is:

$$ T_6 = \binom{8}{5} (x^{-\frac{8}{3}})^{8-5} (x^2 \log_{10} x)^5 $$

Simplifying the powers of $$x$$, we get:

$$ T_6 = \binom{8}{5} (x^{-\frac{8}{3}})^3 (x^2 \log_{10} x)^5 $$

$$ T_6 = \binom{8}{5} x^{-8} (x^{10} (\log_{10} x)^5) $$

Combining like terms:

$$ T_6 = \binom{8}{5} x^{-8 + 10} (\log_{10} x)^5 $$

$$ T_6 = \binom{8}{5} x^2 (\log_{10} x)^5 $$

Given that this term equals 5600:

$$ \binom{8}{5} x^2 (\log_{10} x)^5 = 5600 $$

First, calculate the binomial coefficient $$\binom{8}{5}$$:

$$ \binom{8}{5} = \binom{8}{3} = \frac{8!}{5!3!} = \frac{8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 56 $$

Thus, the equation simplifies to:

$$ 56 x^2 (\log_{10} x)^5 = 5600 $$

Dividing both sides by 56:

$$ x^2 (\log_{10} x)^5 = 100 $$

Let $$\log_{10} x = y$$. Therefore, $$x = 10^y$$ and substituting back into the equation:

$$ (10^y)^2 y^5 = 100 $$

$$ 10^{2y} y^5 = 100 $$

$$ 10^{2y} y^5 = 10^2 $$

Comparing the exponents, we get:

$$ 2y + \log_{10} y^5 = 2 $$

$$ 2y + 5 \log_{10} y = 2 $$

This equation must be solved numerically or through trial and error. However, by inspection and testing values, we find that when $$x = 10$$:

$$ \log_{10} 10 = 1 $$

so

$$ 2 \cdot 1 + 5 \cdot \log_{10} 1 = 2 $$

which simplifies to:

$$ 2 \cdot 1 + 5 \cdot 0 = 2 $$

Hence, the value of $$x$$ is 10.

The correct option is: Option D: 10

questionText: How many terms are there in the expansion of (3x - y)<sup>4</sup>(x + 3y)<sup>4</sup> ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given Expression:

$ (3x - y)^4 (x + 3y)^4 $

First, we simplify:

$ (3x - y)(x + 3y) $

Expanding the product:

$ = 3x^2 + 9xy - xy - 3y^2 = 3x^2 + 8xy - 3y^2 $

Thus, the expression becomes:

$ (3x^2 + 8xy - 3y^2)^4 $

Multinomial Expansion:

The expression $(3x^2 + 8xy - 3y^2)^4$ is a multinomial expansion. To find the number of distinct terms in the expansion, we use the general formula for the number of terms in the expansion of $(a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_m)^n$:

$ \text{Number of terms} = \binom{n + m - 1}{m - 1} $

Here, $n = 4$ (the exponent) and $m = 3$ (the number of different terms: $3x^2$, $8xy$, and $-3y^2$).

Plugging in the values:

$ \text{Number of terms} = \binom{4 + 3 - 1}{3 - 1} = \binom{6}{2} $

Calculating $\binom{6}{2}$:

$ \binom{6}{2} = \frac{6!}{2!(6-2)!} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2 \times 1} = 15 $

Conclusion

Therefore, the number of distinct terms in the expansion of $(3x - y)^4 (x + 3y)^4$ is:

$ \boxed{15} $

questionText:
Consider the following statements for a fixed natural number n:

1. C(n, r) is greatest if n = 2r

2. C(n, r) is greatest if n = 2r - 1 and n = 2r + 1 

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To determine the correctness of the statements about the binomial coefficient $ C(n, r) $ or $ \binom{n}{r} $, let's apply the concepts of binomial coefficients:

Binomial Coefficient $ \binom{n}{r} $:

The binomial coefficient $ \binom{n}{r} $ represents the number of ways to choose $ r $ elements from $ n $ elements without regard to the order. It is given by:

$ \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} $

Key Concepts:

  1. When $ n $ is even:
  • The greatest binomial coefficient occurs at $ r = \frac{n}{2} $.

  • For $ n = 2r $, the binomial coefficient $ \binom{n}{r} $ is maximized.
  1. When $ n $ is odd:
  • The greatest binomial coefficient occurs at $ r = \frac{n-1}{2} $ and $ r = \frac{n+1}{2} $.

  • For $ n = 2r - 1 $ and $ n = 2r + 1 $, the binomial coefficient $ \binom{n}{r} $ is maximized.

Verification of Statements:

  1. Statement 1: $ C(n, r) $ is greatest if $ n = 2r $.
  • This means $ n $ is even, and the maximum binomial coefficient occurs at $ r = \frac{n}{2} $.

  • This statement is correct.
  1. Statement 2: $ C(n, r) $ is greatest if $ n = 2r - 1 $ and $ n = 2r + 1 $.
  • This means $ n $ is odd, and the maximum binomial coefficient occurs at $ r = \frac{n-1}{2} $ or $ r = \frac{n+1}{2} $.

  • This statement is also correct.

Conclusion:

Both statements are correct. Therefore, the correct answer is:

$ \boxed{\text{Option C: Both 1 and 2}} $

questionText: What is the area of minor segment ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the area of the minor segment of the circle defined by the equation $$(x - a)^2 + y^2 = a^2$$ partitioned by the line $$y = x$$, we need to follow these steps:

1. Identify the points of intersection:

The line $$y = x$$ intersects the circle at points where both equations hold true.

Substitute $$y = x$$ into the circle's equation:

$$ (x - a)^2 + x^2 = a^2 $$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$ x^2 - 2ax + a^2 + x^2 = a^2 $$

$$ 2x^2 - 2ax = 0 $$

$$ 2x(x - a) = 0 $$

Thus, the solutions are $$ x = 0 $$ and $$ x = a $$, giving the points of intersection as $$(0,0)$$ and $$(a, a)$$.

2. Calculate the area of the minor segment:

The circle has a radius of $$ a $$ and subtended by the angle at the center formed with the line $$y = x$$.

The total area of the circle is:

$$ \pi a^2 $$

The sector area subtended by the angle $$ \pi/2 $$ is:

$$ \frac{1}{4} \pi a^2 $$

Now, we need to calculate the area of the triangular part under the line $$y = x$$ from $$ (0,0) $$ to $$ (a, a) $$ which is a right triangle.

The area of this right triangle is:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \cdot a \cdot a = \frac{a^2}{2} $$

The area of the minor segment is the area of the sector minus the area of the triangular part:

$$ \frac{1}{4} \pi a^2 - \frac{a^2}{2} $$

Simplifying the expression:

$$ \frac{\pi a^2}{4} - \frac{2a^2}{4} = \frac{(\pi - 2) a^2}{4} $$

Therefore, the area of the minor segment is:

Option A $$ \frac{(\pi - 2) a^2}{4} $$

questionText: What is the maximum value of y?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Minima and Maxima of a Function: These represent the points where the first derivative (slope) of the function equals zero.

To determine the minima or maxima of a function $ f(x) $, follow these steps:

Step 1: Calculate the first derivative $ f'(x) $ of the function.

Step 2: Set the first derivative equal to zero and solve for the critical points (e.g., $ x_1 $, $ x_2 $).

Step 3: Find the second derivative $ f''(x) $ and evaluate its sign at the critical points.

Step 4: If $ f''(x) < 0 $, the function has a maximum at that point; if $ f''(x) > 0 $, it has a minimum at that point.

Calculation:

Given: $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $

To find the maximum $ y $:

$ y = \sqrt{9 - 4x^2} $

Differentiate with respect to $ x $:

$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-8x}{2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2}} $

Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical points:

$ \frac{-8x}{2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2}} = 0 $

This implies:

$ x = 0 $

To confirm $ y $ is a maximum at $ x = 0 $, compute the second derivative:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{-4\sqrt{9 - 4x^2} - 32x^2 / (2 \sqrt{9 - 4x^2})}{(9 - 4x^2)} $

At $ x = 0 $:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{-4 \times 3}{9^{3/2}} = -\frac{4}{3} $

Since $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} < 0$, $ y $ has a maximum at $ x = 0 $.

Thus, the maximum value of $ y $ is:

$ y = \sqrt{9 - 4(0)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3 $

questionText: What is the maximum value of xy ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the maximum value of $ xy $ given the equation $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $, Lagrange multipliers can be used. Define the function $ f(x,y) = xy $ and the constraint $ g(x,y) = 4x^2 + y^2 - 9 = 0 $.

Using Lagrange multipliers:

Set up the Lagrangian: $$ \mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = xy + \lambda (9 - 4x^2 - y^2) $$

Take the partial derivatives with respect to $ x $, $ y $, and $ \lambda $: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = y - 8x\lambda = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = x - 2y\lambda = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = 9 - 4x^2 - y^2 = 0 $$

Solve the system of equations: From $ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 0 $: $$ y = 8x\lambda $$

From $ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 0 $: $$ x = 2y\lambda $$

Substitute $ y = 8x\lambda $ into $ x = 2y\lambda $: $$ x = 2(8x\lambda)\lambda $$ $$ x = 16x\lambda^2 $$ $$ 1 = 16\lambda^2 $$ $$ \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{16} $$ $$ \lambda = \pm \frac{1}{4} $$

Consider $ \lambda = \frac{1}{4} $: $$ y = 8x\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 2x $$

Substitute $ y = 2x $ into the constraint $ 4x^2 + y^2 = 9 $: $$ 4x^2 + (2x)^2 = 9 $$ $$ 4x^2 + 4x^2 = 9 $$ $$ 8x^2 = 9 $$ $$ x^2 = \frac{9}{8} $$ $$ x = \pm \frac{3}{2\sqrt{2}} = \pm \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4} $$

For $ x = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4} $: $$ y = 2 \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}\right) = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

Now calculate $ xy $: $$ xy = \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}\right) \left(\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = \frac{9 \cdot 2}{8} = \frac{18}{8} = \frac{9}{4} $$

Therefore, the maximum value of $ xy $ is $ \boxed{\frac{9}{4}} $.

questionText: What is the area between the curve f(x) = x |x| and x-axis for x = [-1, 1]?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$, we need to evaluate the definite integral of $ |x| \cdot x $ from $-1$ to $1$:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} f(x) \, dx = \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx $$

Breaking it down into two integrals over the intervals $[-1, 0]$ and $[0, 1]$:

For $x \in [-1, 0]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = -x $:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x|x| \, dx = \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx $$

For $x \in [0, 1]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = x $:

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx $$

Calculate each integral:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{0} = \left( \frac{0^3}{3} - \frac{(-1)^3}{3} \right) = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} $$

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = \left( \frac{1^3}{3} - \frac{0^3}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} - 0 = \frac{1}{3} $$

Add the results:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} $$

Hence, the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$ is:

$$ \boxed{\frac{2}{3}} $$
